papergraph.title: Prediction of PLX-4720 Sensitivity in Cancer Cell Lines through Multi-Omics Integration and Attention-Based Fusion Modeling
papergraph.citation: La Ode Aman et al. (2025) Prediction of PLX-4720 Sensitivity in Cancer Cell Lines through Multi-Omics Integration and Attention-Based Fusion Modeling arXiv:2512.12113v1
papergraph.abstract: Predicting the sensitivity of cancer cell lines to PLX-4720, a preclinical BRAF inhibitor, requires models capable of capturing the multilayered regulation of oncogenic signaling. Single-omics predictors are often insufficient because drug response is shaped by interactions among genomic alterations, epigenetic regulation, transcriptional activity, protein signaling, metabolic state, and network-level context. In this study we develop an attention-based multi-omics integration framework using genomic, epigenomic, transcriptomic, proteomic, metabolomic, and protein interaction data from the GDSC1 panel. Each modality is encoded into a latent representation using feed-forward neural networks or graph convolutional networks, and fused through an attention mechanism that assigns modality-specific importance weights. A regression model is then used to predict PLX-4720 response. Across single- and multi-omics configurations, the best performance is achieved by integrating genomics and transcriptomics, which yields validation R2 values above 0.92. This reflects the complementary roles of mutational status and downstream transcriptional activation in shaping sensitivity to BRAF inhibition. Epigenomics is the strongest single-omics predictor, while metabolomics and PPI data contribute additional context when combined with other modalities. Integration of three to five omics layers improves stability but does not surpass the accuracy of the best two-modality combinations, likely due to information redundancy and sample-size imbalance. These findings highlight the importance of modality selection rather than maximal data depth. The proposed framework provides an efficient and biologically grounded strategy for drug response prediction and supports the development of precision pharmacogenomics.
papergraph.full_text_excerpt:
[TITLE]
Prediction of PLX-4720 Sensitivity in Cancer Cell Lines through Multi-Omics Integration and Attention-Based Fusion Modeling

[PAPER_TYPE]
mechanism

[PRIORITY_SECTIONS]
[SECTION: METHODS | pages 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]
This study is an in silico investigation employing a retrospective observational design based
on pharmacogenomic data from cancer cell lines.
Drug sensitivity data and multi-omics
molecular profiles were obtained from the Genomics of Drug Sensitivity in Cancer
(GDSC) database, which provides drug response information and multiple layers of
molecular data for hundreds of cancer cell lines across diverse tumor types (Iorio et al.,
2016; Yang et al., 2013).
The analysis focused on PLX-4720 (GDSC1:1371), a preclinical BRAF inhibitor.
consisted of cancer cell lines with available PLX-4720 response data as well as
proteomic, metabolomic, and
protein–protein interaction (PPI) profiles.
Only cell lines with complete data or, at minimum,
valid features–label pairs for PLX-4720 were included in the analysis.
These values were used as continuous targets for regression-based drug sensitivity
modeling, reflecting the degree of cell line sensitivity to PLX-4720 (Iorio et al., 2016).
Genomic data comprised profiles of single nucleotide variants (SNVs) and small
into continuous features per cell line, following e
...[truncated]

[SECTION: DISCUSSION | pages 15, 16, 17]
The results demonstrate that PLX-4720 sensitivity can be accurately predicted using a
multi-omics integration approach; however, improvements in accuracy are not linear with the
number of combined modalities. Although stepwise integration enriches the underlying
biological context, the best performance is achieved through the integration of two specific
modalities—namely genomics + transcriptomics. This configuration reaches an R² above
0.92, representing the highest accuracy among all tested models.
Biological Interpretation of Two-Modality Integration
The strong performance of the genomics + transcriptomics combination reflects the
mechanism of action of PLX-4720 as a BRAF inhibitor. Response to PLX-4720 is influenced
by:
●​ upstream mutation status in BRAF or RAS (genomics), and​
●​ downstream pathway activation (transcriptomics).
Mutations alone cannot fully explain signaling rewiring without considering gene expression,
while gene expression without mutational context may obscure driver-related effects. Thus,
integrating
these
two
regulatory
layers
captures
MAPK
pathway
status
more
comprehensively, consistent with principles of oncogene addiction and sensitivity to BRAF
inhibitors.
Role of Other Modalities
Epigenomics emerges as the strongest single-modality predictor, indicating that DNA
methylation contributes to heterogeneity in drug response. When combined with
transcriptomics, proteomics, or PPI data, epigenomics offers moderate accuracy gains,
reflecting its role in regulating gene expression and protein activity states.
Metabolomics and PPI data have limited predictive value on their own but enhance accuracy
when integrated into two- to five-layer models. These non-transcriptomic modalities may
serve as biochemical and network-level contextual enhancers within the predictive
framework.
Diminishing Returns in Higher-Level Integration
Although integrating five modalities yields the most comprehensive biological representation,
predictive accuracy does not surpass that of the best two-modality combinations. This
phenomenon may arise from feature redundancy, increased noise, and sample-size
imbalance across modalities. When additional layers do not contribute independent signal,
model complexity increases without corresponding gains in inference capability.
Methodologically, these findings emphasize that selecting the right modalities is more
important than maximizing the number of modalities in pharmacogenomic modeling.
Implications for Pharmacogenomics
Models
integrating
two
complementary
omics
layers—such
as
genomics
+
transcriptomics—offer substantial practical value because they:
(a) deliver maximal predictive accuracy,​
(b) require fewer laboratory assays, and​
(c) maintain lower model complexity than five-modality integrations.
These results imply that a selective, modality-focused strategy may be more efficient than
extensive multi-omics integration for predicting drug response, prioritizing cell lines, or
generating hypotheses regarding resistance mechanisms.
Limitations and Future Directions
The sample size is relatively small (18–28 cell lines per configuration), making models
susceptible to variability. This limitation is common in pharmacogenomic studies of cancer
cell lines and highlights the need for external validation (Wang et al., 2021). Additionally, the
dataset
consists
of
baseline static molecular profiles, without post-treatment
dynamics—despite the fact that resistance to BRAF inhibitors is often adaptive. Integrating
dynamic or time-course omics could improve interpretability.
Although deep learning models are traditionally viewed as "black boxes," attention
mechanisms and interpretability tools such as SHAP can help link predictions to key
biological features (Zhang et al., 2021). For future research, several logical directions
include:
●​ incorporating drug chemical structure, as implemented in PASO (Wu et al., 2025),
●​ leveraging transfer learning or multitask learning (Uyar et al., 2021),
●​ integrating pathway-level or network module information (Zhang et al., 2021).
Overall, this study reinforces attention-based multi-omics integration as a relevant and
powerful strategy for modeling PLX-4720 sensitivity, further solidifying multidimensional
omics as a foundational pillar of precision pharmacogenomics.
CONCLUSION
This study demonstrates that PLX-4720 sensitivity in cancer cell lines can be accurately
predicted using a multi-omics integration approach. Predictive performance is not
determined solely by the number of modalities combined, but rather by the biologically
relevant combinations of layers. Integration of two modalities—particularly genomics and
transcriptomics—produces the most accurate models, achieving validation R² values above
0.92 and outperforming all three-, four-, and five-modality configurations. These findings
highlight that driver mutation status and transcriptional activation of the MAPK pathway are
key determinants of PLX-4720 sensitivity, consistent with the mechanism of BRAF inhibition.
Epigenomics stands out as the most informative single modality, underscoring the
importance of epigenetic regulation in drug response heterogeneity. While integrating three
to five omics layers improves stability and biological context, it does not always enhance
predictive accuracy due to potential information redundancy, differences in data depth, or
cross-modality sample imbalance. Therefore, careful modality selection is more important
than simply increasing the number of omics layers.
Practically, these results have important implications for precision pharmacogenomics. Drug
response prediction models do not require extensive multi-modality integration; instead,
combining two complementary layers can deliver maximal performance with lower data
requirements and reduced experimental costs. This study supports the use of
genomics–transcriptomics integration as an efficient strategy for prioritizing cell lines,
identifying oncogene dependencies, and accelerating predictive analyses for BRAF inhibitor
response.

papergraph.conclusion: This study demonstrates that PLX-4720 sensitivity in cancer cell lines can be accurately predicted using a multi-omics integration approach. Predictive performance is not determined solely by the number of modalities combined, but rather by the biologically relevant combinations of layers. Integration of two modalities—particularly genomics and transcriptomics—produces the most accurate models, achieving validation R² values above 0.92 and outperforming all three-, four-, and five-modality configurations. These findings highlight that driver mutation status and transcriptional activation of the MAPK pathway are key determinants of PLX-4720 sensitivity, consistent with the mechanism of BRAF inhibition. Epigenomics stands out as the most informative single modality, underscoring the importance of epigenetic regulation in drug response heterogeneity. While integrating three to five omics layers improves stability and biological context, it does not always enhance predictive accuracy due to potential information redundancy, differences in data depth, or cross-modality sample imbalance. Therefore, careful modality selection is more important than simply increasing the number of omics layers. Practically, these results have important implications for precision pharmacogenomics. Drug response prediction models do not require extensive multi-modality integration; instead, combining two complementary layers can deliver maximal performance with lower data requirements and reduced experimental costs. This study supports the use of genomics–transcriptomics integration as an efficient strategy for prioritizing cell lines, identifying oncogene dependencies, and accelerating predictive analyses for BRAF inhibitor response.

papergraph.title: A novel approach to differential expression analysis of co-occurrence networks for small-sampled microbiome data
papergraph.citation: Nandini Gadhia et al. (2024) A novel approach to differential expression analysis of co-occurrence networks for small-sampled microbiome data arXiv:2412.03744v1
papergraph.abstract: Graph-based machine learning methods are useful tools in the identification and prediction of variation in genetic data. In particular, the comprehension of phenotypic effects at the cellular level is an accelerating research area in pharmacogenomics. In this article, a novel graph theoretic approach is proposed to infer a co-occurrence network from 16S microbiome data. The approach is specialised to handle datasets containing a small number of samples. Small datasets exacerbate the significant challenges faced by biological data, which exhibit properties such as sparsity, compositionality, and complexity of interactions. Methodologies are also proposed to enrich and statistically filter the inferred networks. The utility of the proposed method lies in that it extracts an informative network from small sampled data that is not only feature-rich, but also biologically meaningful and statistically significant. Although specialised for small data sets, which are abundant, it can be generally applied to any small-sampled dataset, and can also be extended to integrate multi-omics data. The proposed methodology is tested on a data set of chickens vaccinated against and challenged by the protozoan parasite Eimeria tenella. The raw genetic reads are processed, and networks inferred to describe the ecosystems of the chicken intestines under three different stages of disease progression. Analysis of the expression of network features derive biologically intuitive conclusions from purely statistical methods. For example, there is a clear evolution in the distribution of node features in line with the progression of the disease. The distributions also reveal clusters of species interacting mutualistically and parasitically, as expected. Moreover, a specific sub-network is found to persist through all experimental conditions, representative of a persistent microbiome.

papergraph.title: A Patient-specific Computational Model for Neonates and Infants with Borderline Left Ventricles
papergraph.citation: Chen, Y. et al. (2025) A Patient-specific Computational Model for Neonates and Infants with Borderline Left Ventricles doi:10.1101/2025.07.15.25331596
papergraph.abstract: PurposeBorderline left ventricle (BLV) presents a dilemma between pursuing a biventricular repair (BiVR) and a Stage 1 palliation (S1P) because a discordant pursuit of BiVR increases mortality risk. We aim to develop and validate a personalized computational model to assist surgical decision-making by predicting virtual surgery hemodynamics in BLV patients. MethodsWe developed a novel multi-block lumped parameter network (LPN) model of a BLV circulatory system. Patient-specific model parameters were estimated using a semi-automatic tuning framework to fit clinical data in ten retrospectively identified BLV patients. Virtual surgeries (BiVR and S1P) were performed on each patient to quantify post-operative hemodynamics. ResultsIn patients who clinically received S1P (Group I, N=5), a virtual BiVR predicted significantly elevated mean pulmonary artery pressure (PAPmean: 38.00{+/-}10.0 vs. 17.50{+/-}2.7 mmHg, p<0.01), mean left atrial pressure (LAPmean: 25.40{+/-}8.2 vs. 6.20{+/-}1.2 mmHg, p<0.0001), and single ventricle end-diastolic pressure (SVEDP: 21.80{+/-}8.7 vs. 4.80{+/-}1.3 mmHg, p<0.0001) compared with a virtual S1P. A virtual BiVR in patients who clinically underwent BiVR (Group II, N=5) did not predict any adverse hemodynamic outcome. ConclusionsA novel digital twinning framework was developed to predict hemodynamics following virtual surgeries in BLV patients. The model predictions align with the clinically adopted procedure in this retrospectively selected cohort by predicting unacceptable PAP, LAP, and SVEDP. This predictive tool may guide surgeons in determining the hemodynamically optimal surgery for BLV infants, but it needs prospective validation.
papergraph.conclusion: A novel digital twinning framework was developed to predict hemodynamics following virtual surgeries in BLV patients. The model predictions align with the clinically adopted procedure in this retrospectively selected cohort by predicting unacceptable PAP, LAP, and SVEDP. This predictive tool may guide surgeons in determining the hemodynamically optimal surgery for BLV infants, but it needs prospective validation.

evidence.title: Interfacing data science with cell therapy manufacturing: where we are and where we need to be.

evidence.limitation: Challenges include starting material and patient variability, limited understanding of manufacturing process parameter effects on quality, complex supply chain logistics, and lack of predictive, well-understood product quality attributes.

hypotheses.statement: If the competing claims about decentralized manufacturing of cell and gene therapy products are evaluated under matched the experimental, observational, or simulation conditions that differ between the claims conditions, then AUC scores, sensitivity, specificity, Dice scores, K-statistic will separate which mechanism holds and identify the boundary condition the condition where the competing explanations diverge. The decisive test is constrained to AUC∞, Cmax, t1/2, adverse drug reaction (ADR) occurrence under an explicit stress regime.
hypotheses.mechanism: Concrete mechanism chain: (1) method capability: 16S (V3-V4) rRNA sequencing, Kaplan-Meier and Cox proportional hazard models contributes through testing mechanistic predictions under controlled parameter variations; (2) scenario target: in decentralized manufacturing of cell and gene therapy products, the affected process is a measurable biological or clinical mechanism such as expression, pathway activation, response, adverse effect, or persistence; (3) measurable consequence: the bridge must produce a preregistered change in AUC scores, sensitivity, specificity, Dice scores, K-statistic. The method's input requirements are broad or not clearly specified; the experiment must make them explicit. The required bridge is multi-omics. The decisive prediction is that this concrete bridge, not a generic representation change, will alter AUC scores, sensitivity, specificity, Dice scores, K-statistic; if the bridge data or causal link is absent, the hypothesis should fail rather than be reinterpreted post hoc.
hypotheses.expected_value: Contradiction gaps are high-value because resolving them can update mechanism understanding, not merely fill a sparse method-scenario cell.
hypotheses.test_plan: Build a minimal benchmark for decentralized manufacturing of cell and gene therapy products; compare 16S (V3-V4) rRNA sequencing, Kaplan-Meier and Cox proportional hazard models against canonical baselines; measure AUC scores, sensitivity, specificity, Dice scores, K-statistic; include negative controls, ablations, and failure-mode analysis. Add a preregistered stress test for AUC∞, Cmax, t1/2, adverse drug reaction (ADR) occurrence.

issues: Method-scenario semantic bridge is under-specified: bridge concepts detected: multi-omics
suggestions: Add the missing bridge representation before treating the hypothesis as plausible.

semantic_plausibility.reason: bridge concepts detected: multi-omics

hypotheses.competition_advantage: Ranks well because it is traceable to a high-value gap, has an explicit mechanism, passes generic disciplinary plausibility checks, and includes falsifiable validation criteria.

verification_plan.primary_metric: AUC scores, sensitivity, specificity, Dice scores, K-statistic
verification_plan.falsification_condition: No directional, non-monotonic, or mechanism-separating change in AUC scores, sensitivity, specificity, Dice scores, K-statistic when the experimental, observational, or simulation conditions that differ between the claims crosses the condition where the competing explanations diverge.

counterfactual_experiments.question: Does changing the experimental, observational, or simulation conditions that differ between the claims alter AUC scores, sensitivity, specificity, Dice scores, K-statistic through the proposed mediator, rather than only correlating with it?
counterfactual_experiments.design: Compare matched control and intervention conditions that vary the experimental, observational, or simulation conditions that differ between the claims while measuring the proposed mediator and AUC scores, sensitivity, specificity, Dice scores, K-statistic; include a negative control that leaves the experimental, observational, or simulation conditions that differ between the claims unchanged.
counterfactual_experiments.predicted_outcome_if_mechanism_true: The intervention changes the proposed mediator before or together with a reproducible directional change in AUC scores, sensitivity, specificity, Dice scores, K-statistic.
counterfactual_experiments.predicted_outcome_if_mechanism_false: AUC scores, sensitivity, specificity, Dice scores, K-statistic does not change systematically when the experimental, observational, or simulation conditions that differ between the claims is varied, or it changes without the predicted mediator response.

keynotes.title: The effects of genetic polymorphism on toxicity and pharmacokinetics of methotrexate in Egyptian adult patients with leukaemia or lymphoma

keynote.title: The effects of genetic polymorphism on toxicity and pharmacokinetics of methotrexate in Egyptian adult patients with leukaemia or lymphoma
keynote.core_problem: Investigate the influence of specific genetic polymorphisms (MTHFR C677T, DHFR19 Ins/del, GGH −401 C > T, MTR A2756G) on methotrexate (MTX) toxicity and pharmacokinetics in Egyptian patients with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) or Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma (NHL).
contributions: Identified the impact of MTHFR C677T, DHFR19 Ins/del, GGH −401 C > T, and MTR A2756G polymorphisms on MTX toxicity and pharmacokinetics in Egyptian patients with ALL or NHL.
methods: Prospectively enrolled 50 adult Egyptian patients with ALL and NHL treated with high-dose MTX.
Collected clinical and biochemical data from medical records after each cycle of MTX.
Measured plasma concentrations of MTX 72 hours after the initiation of infusion.
Performed genotyping using PCR-ARMS and PCR-RFLP assays.
experiments_or_evidence: MTHFR C677T T variants significantly increased the risk of leukopenia, and the TT genotype was associated with lymphocytopenia, thrombocytopenia, and anemia.
GGH-401 TT genotype was significantly correlated with anemia.
Plasma MTX levels were significantly higher in patients with MTR A2756G G variants.

gap_signals.text: Pharmacogenetic testing is recommended to optimize MTX therapy.

datasets_or_materials: Clinical and biochemical data from 50 adult Egyptian patients with ALL and NHL.

important_claims.claim: MTHFR C677T T variants increase the risk of leukopenia.
important_claims.evidence: MTHFR C677T T variants significantly increased the risk of leukopenia.

important_claims.claim: MTHFR 677 C > T TT genotype is associated with lymphocytopenia, thrombocytopenia, and anemia.
important_claims.evidence: The genotype MTHFR 677 C > T TT significantly associated with lymphocytopenia, thrombocytopenia, and anaemia.

important_claims.claim: GGH-401 TT genotype is correlated with anemia.
important_claims.evidence: The genotype GGH-401 TT was significantly correlated with anaemia.

important_claims.claim: Plasma MTX level is higher in patients with MTR A2756G G variants.
important_claims.evidence: Plasma MTX level was significantly higher in patients with MTR A2756G G variants.

steps.claim: MTHFR C677T T variants increase the risk of leukopenia.
steps.evidence: MTHFR C677T T variants significantly increased the risk of leukopenia.

causal_chains.outcome: Increased risk of leukopenia

steps.claim: MTHFR 677 C > T TT genotype is associated with lymphocytopenia, thrombocytopenia, and anemia.
steps.evidence: The genotype MTHFR 677 C > T TT significantly associated with lymphocytopenia, thrombocytopenia, and anaemia.

causal_chains.outcome: Lymphocytopenia, thrombocytopenia, and anemia

steps.claim: GGH-401 TT genotype is correlated with anemia.
steps.evidence: The genotype GGH-401 TT was significantly correlated with anaemia.

causal_chains.outcome: Anemia

steps.claim: Plasma MTX level is higher in patients with MTR A2756G G variants.
steps.evidence: Plasma MTX level was significantly higher in patients with MTR A2756G G variants.

causal_chains.outcome: Higher plasma MTX level

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides valuable insights into the role of specific genetic polymorphisms in the toxicity and pharmacokinetics of methotrexate, which can be used to inform personalized treatment strategies for patients with ALL or NHL. The findings support the need for pharmacogenetic testing to optimize MTX therapy.

keynotes.title: Genetic variants and clinical factors affecting the response to 5-fluorouracil–based treatment in Chilean patients with advanced colorectal cancer.

keynote.title: Genetic variants and clinical factors affecting the response to 5-fluorouracil–based treatment in Chilean patients with advanced colorectal cancer
keynote.core_problem: The response to 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) based treatment for colorectal cancer varies greatly among Chilean patients, influenced by genetic polymorphisms and clinical factors. There is a lack of comprehensive predictive models that combine these variables to predict chemotherapy safety.
contributions: Identification of genetic variants that affect 5-FU treatment response
Development of multivariate models to predict chemotherapy safety
limitations: The study is an initial step, and more work is needed to develop comprehensive predictive models

gap_signals.text: The study is an initial step, and more work is needed to develop comprehensive predictive models
no comprehensive model combining genetic and clinical variables to predict chemotherapy safety in Chilean CRC patients has been developed

important_claims.claim: Patient responses to 5-FU vary greatly due to genetic polymorphisms in enzymes like thymidylate synthase (TYMS) and dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD)
important_claims.evidence: Polymorphisms in genes related to oxaliplatin elimination and efficacy, such as glutathione-S-transferases (GSTs) and DNA repair enzymes, also play a role.

steps.claim: Variations in thymidylate synthase (TYMS) and dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) affect the drug's pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics
steps.evidence: Patient responses to 5-FU vary greatly due to genetic polymorphisms in enzymes like thymidylate synthase (TYMS) and dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD), which affect the drug's pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics.

causal_chains.outcome: Variable patient response to 5-FU treatment
interventions: Identifying and understanding genetic polymorphisms in TYMS and DPD

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The findings can be used to develop pharmacogenetic-based dose adjustment models aimed at reducing adverse drug reactions (ADRs) in Chilean CRC patients undergoing 5-FU-based treatment. This could lead to personalized medicine approaches and improved treatment outcomes.

keynotes.title: Pharmacogenetics of antidepressant response: a focused review on CYP2C19, CYP2D6, SLC6A4, and HTR2A polymorphisms

keynote.title: Pharmacogenetics of antidepressant response: a focused review on CYP2C19, CYP2D6, SLC6A4, and HTR2A polymorphisms
keynote.core_problem: Understanding the impact of genetic variability on the efficacy and safety of antidepressants, particularly selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs).
contributions: Synthesizing evidence on how pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic polymorphisms influence antidepressant therapy.
Highlighting the role of CYP2C19, CYP2D6, SLC6A4, and HTR2A in guiding personalized antidepressant treatment.
methods: Comprehensive literature search in NCBI and Google Scholar (2017-2024) for meta-analyses and clinical studies on genotype-phenotype associations in SSRI-treated patients.
experiments_or_evidence: Meta-analyses and clinical studies evaluating the impact of CYP2C19, CYP2D6, SLC6A4, and HTR2A polymorphisms on SSRI efficacy and safety.
assumptions: Genetic variability significantly influences drug metabolism and therapeutic outcomes.
limitations: The review is limited to the years 2017-2024 and may not include earlier or more recent findings.
Focus is primarily on SSRIs, which may limit generalizability to other types of antidepressants.

gap_signals.text: Focus is primarily on SSRIs, which may limit generalizability to other types of antidepressants.
The review is limited to the years 2017-2024 and may not include earlier or more recent findings.
Integration of these genetic markers into clinical frameworks for more rational antidepressant selection and dosing.

datasets_or_materials: NCBI and Google Scholar databases

important_claims.claim: CYP2C19 and CYP2D6 polymorphisms markedly affect plasma concentrations, therapeutic outcomes, and adverse-event risk.
important_claims.evidence: Findings from meta-analyses and clinical studies indicate that poor metabolizers exhibit increased efficacy but greater toxicity, while ultrarapid metabolizers show reduced therapeutic response.

important_claims.claim: SLC6A4 (5-HTTLPR) and HTR2A polymorphisms modulate serotonin transporter availability and receptor sensitivity, influencing clinical improvement and tolerability.
important_claims.evidence: Functional variants such as SLC6A4 and HTR2A, especially in interaction with environmental stressors, affect clinical outcomes and tolerability.

steps.claim: Poor metabolizers have increased efficacy but greater toxicity.
steps.evidence: Findings from meta-analyses and clinical studies indicate that poor metabolizers exhibit increased efficacy but greater toxicity.

steps.claim: Ultrarapid metabolizers show reduced therapeutic response.
steps.evidence: Findings from meta-analyses and clinical studies indicate that ultrarapid metabolizers show reduced therapeutic response.

causal_chains.outcome: Marked effect on plasma concentrations, therapeutic outcomes, and adverse-event risk.
interventions: Personalized dosing based on genetic markers

steps.claim: Modulate serotonin transporter availability and receptor sensitivity.
steps.evidence: Functional variants such as SLC6A4 and HTR2A modulate serotonin transporter availability and receptor sensitivity.

steps.claim: Influence clinical improvement and tolerability, especially in interaction with environmental stressors.
steps.evidence: Especially in interaction with environmental stressors, these variants affect clinical outcomes and tolerability.

causal_chains.outcome: Influencing clinical improvement and tolerability.
interventions: Incorporating genetic markers into clinical decision-making

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive synthesis of the current understanding of how specific genetic polymorphisms (CYP2C19, CYP2D6, SLC6A4, and HTR2A) influence the efficacy and safety of SSRIs. It can serve as a foundational reference for future research on personalized medicine approaches in psychiatry and the development of more targeted and effective antidepressant therapies.

keynotes.title: The Three Musketeers in Cancer Therapy: Pharmacokinetics, Pharmacodynamics and Personalised Approach

keynote.title: The Three Musketeers in Cancer Therapy: Pharmacokinetics, Pharmacodynamics and Personalised Approach
keynote.core_problem: Cancer therapy is moving from a one-size-fits-all approach to personalized treatments, but faces challenges such as high costs, insufficient infrastructure, and complex regulatory processes.
contributions: Advancing personalized cancer therapy through pharmacokinetic (PK) and pharmacodynamic (PD) considerations, innovative drug delivery systems, and rational combination regimens, leading to improved efficacy and reduced toxicity.
limitations: High cost of biomarker testing
Insufficient laboratory infrastructure
Inconsistent reimbursement policies
Operational inefficiencies such as long turnaround times or lack of clinician awareness
Complex regulatory processes, particularly around the co-development of targeted drugs and companion diagnostics
Evidentiary requirements for rare subgroups

gap_signals.text: Complex regulatory processes, particularly around the co-development of targeted drugs and companion diagnostics
Evidentiary requirements for rare subgroups
High cost of biomarker testing
Inconsistent reimbursement policies
Insufficient laboratory infrastructure
Operational inefficiencies such as long turnaround times or lack of clinician awareness
Key barriers include the high cost of biomarker testing, insufficient laboratory infrastructure, and inconsistent reimbursement policies.
Regulatory processes also remain complex, particularly around the co-development of targeted drugs and companion diagnostics, and the evidentiary requirements for rare subgroups.

important_claims.claim: Precision oncology tailors prevention, diagnosis, and treatment to the individual patient’s genetic and molecular profile, improving efficacy and reducing toxicity.
important_claims.evidence: Key advancements underscore this shift: molecularly targeted drugs (e.g., trastuzumab for HER2-positive breast cancer, EGFR and ALK inhibitors for lung cancer) have improved efficacy and reduced toxicity compared to conventional therapy.

important_claims.claim: Pharmacokinetic (PK) and pharmacodynamic (PD) considerations are central to personalizing treatment, explaining variability in drug exposure and response among patients and guiding dose optimization.
important_claims.evidence: Modern strategies like therapeutic drug monitoring and model-informed precision dosing seek to maintain drug levels in the therapeutic range, improving outcomes.

important_claims.claim: Immunotherapies, including checkpoint inhibitors and CAR-T cells, have transformed oncology, though patient selection via biomarkers (such as PD-L1 expression or tumor mutational burden) is critical to identify likely responders.
important_claims.evidence: Innovative drug delivery systems, notably nanomedicine, address PK challenges by enhancing tumor-specific drug accumulation and enabling novel therapeutics.

steps.claim: Tailoring treatment to the individual patient’s genetic and molecular profile
steps.evidence: Precision oncology tailors prevention, diagnosis, and treatment to the individual patient’s genetic and molecular profile.

steps.claim: Improved efficacy and reduced toxicity
steps.evidence: Molecularly targeted drugs (e.g., trastuzumab for HER2-positive breast cancer, EGFR and ALK inhibitors for lung cancer) have improved efficacy and reduced toxicity compared to conventional therapy.

causal_chains.outcome: Better patient outcomes

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and future directions of personalized cancer therapy, highlighting the importance of pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and innovative drug delivery systems. It identifies key barriers and suggests areas for improvement, making it valuable for researchers and policymakers working on advancing personalized medicine.

keynotes.title: Individualization of Irinotecan Treatment: A Review of Pharmacokinetics, Pharmacodynamics, and Pharmacogenetics

keynote.title: Individualization of Irinotecan Treatment: A Review of Pharmacokinetics, Pharmacodynamics, and Pharmacogenetics
keynote.core_problem: Irinotecan therapy is characterized by dose-limiting toxicities and large interindividual pharmacokinetic variability, which can be influenced by genetic and non-genetic factors.
contributions: Provides an updated overview on the current knowledge of irinotecan pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and pharmacogenetics.
methods: Review of existing literature including retrospective and prospective trials, meta-analyses, and guidelines.
experiments_or_evidence: Retrospective and prospective trials, meta-analyses, and clinical observations.
assumptions: Genetic variants in enzymes and transporters involved in irinotecan metabolism can predict drug-related toxicity and efficacy.
limitations: Lack of prospective studies evaluating the clinical effects of genotype-directed dosing, UGT1A1 genotyping is not routinely performed.

gap_signals.text: Lack of prospective studies evaluating the clinical effects of genotype-directed dosing, UGT1A1 genotyping is not routinely performed.
Prospective studies evaluating the clinical effects of genotype-directed dosing are scarce.

datasets_or_materials: Tables 1-4: Drug–drug interactions with irinotecan, Irinotecan toxicity in relation to pharmacokinetics and biliary index, Overview of pharmacogenetic studies on irinotecan toxicity and survival, Overview of guidelines on pharmacogenetic testing for irinotecan.

important_claims.claim: UGT1A1*28 variant alleles are associated with increased SN-38 plasma concentrations and decreased absolute neutrophil counts, leading to higher risk of hematologic toxicities.
important_claims.evidence: Clinically, UGT1A1*93 is associated with increased SN-38 AUC, lower neutrophil count, increased incidence of hematologic toxicities (including neutropenia), diarrhea, and grade 3 vomiting. [256, 257, 258, 259, 260, 261]

important_claims.claim: SLCO1B1*1b variant allele is associated with a protective effect against neutropenia and may improve efficacy.
important_claims.evidence: In Caucasian patients carrying at least one SLCO1B1*1b variant allele, median neutrophil count increased approximately twofold compared with wild-types, presumably by increased hepatic uptake of SN-38, thereby reducing SN-38 plasma concentrations. [273, 274]

steps.claim: Increased SN-38 plasma concentrations
steps.evidence: UGT1A1*28 variant alleles are associated with increased SN-38 AUC. [256]

steps.claim: Decreased absolute neutrophil counts
steps.evidence: UGT1A1*28 variant alleles are associated with decreased absolute neutrophil counts. [256]

causal_chains.outcome: Higher risk of hematologic toxicities, including neutropenia, diarrhea, and grade 3 vomiting
observables: SN-38 AUC, neutrophil count, incidence of hematologic toxicities
interventions: Dose reduction in patients homozygous for UGT1A1*28

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The review provides a comprehensive summary of the current understanding of irinotecan's pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and pharmacogenetics, which can inform future research and clinical practice in individualizing irinotecan treatment to optimize efficacy and reduce toxicity.

keynotes.title: CYP3A4 and CYP3A5: the crucial roles in clinical drug metabolism and the significant implications of genetic polymorphisms

keynote.title: CYP3A4 and CYP3A5: the crucial roles in clinical drug metabolism and the significant implications of genetic polymorphisms
keynote.core_problem: Inter-individual variability in enzymatic activity of CYP3A4/5 due to genetic polymorphisms, leading to different pharmacokinetic profiles and affecting drug efficacy and toxicity.
contributions: Reviewing the role of CYP3A4/5 in drug metabolism and their impact on personalized treatment regimens.
Discussing detection methods, enzymatic kinetic characteristics, and clinical guidelines for CYP3A5.
Summarizing the importance of CYP3A4/5 in clinical applications, including personalized therapy, management of drug-drug interactions, and adjustment of drug doses.

gap_signals.text: The review does not provide specific experimental or methodological details.

important_claims.claim: CYP3A4 and CYP3A5 metabolize approximately 30–50% of known drugs.
important_claims.evidence: CYP3A, a key member of the cytochrome P450 (CYP450) superfamily, is integral to drug metabolism, processing a substantial portion of medications. Their role in drug metabolism is particularly prominent, as CYP3A4 and CYP3A5 metabolize approximately 30–50% of known drugs.

important_claims.claim: Genetic polymorphisms of CYP3A4/5 can lead to inter-individual variability in enzymatic activity, resulting in different pharmacokinetic profiles and requiring dosage adjustments based on genetic profiles.
important_claims.evidence: The genetic polymorphism of CYP3A4/5 is significant inter-individual variability in enzymatic activity, which can result in different pharmacokinetic profiles in response to the same drug among individuals. These polymorphisms can lead to either increased drug toxicity or reduced therapeutic effects, requiring dosage adjustments based on genetic profiles.

steps.claim: Significant inter-individual variability in enzymatic activity
steps.evidence: The genetic polymorphism of CYP3A4/5 is significant inter-individual variability in enzymatic activity, which can result in different pharmacokinetic profiles in response to the same drug among individuals.

steps.claim: Different pharmacokinetic profiles in response to the same drug
steps.evidence: These polymorphisms can lead to either increased drug toxicity or reduced therapeutic effects, requiring dosage adjustments based on genetic profiles.

steps.claim: Increased drug toxicity or reduced therapeutic effects
steps.evidence: These polymorphisms can lead to either increased drug toxicity or reduced therapeutic effects, requiring dosage adjustments based on genetic profiles.

causal_chains.outcome: Necessity for dosage adjustments based on genetic profiles
interventions: Dosage adjustments based on genetic profiles

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive understanding of the functions and genetic characteristics of CYP3A4/5, which can be used to optimize drug therapy and improve clinical outcomes through personalized treatment regimens.

keynotes.title: Response and Toxicity to Cytarabine Therapy in Leukemia and Lymphoma: From Dose Puzzle to Pharmacogenomic Biomarkers

keynote.title: Response and Toxicity to Cytarabine Therapy in Leukemia and Lymphoma: From Dose Puzzle to Pharmacogenomic Biomarkers
keynote.core_problem: Individual variability in clinical response to cytarabine (Ara-C) therapy, which can lead to suboptimal outcomes, is associated with intracellular accumulation of Ara-CTP due to genetic variants related to metabolic enzymes.
contributions: Report on the latest pharmacogenomic biomarkers associated with the response to cytarabine
Discuss new drug formulations with optimized pharmacokinetics
experiments_or_evidence: Studies suggest that individual variability in clinical response to leukemia and lymphoma treatments is linked to genetic variants affecting metabolic enzyme function, leading to differential intracellular accumulation of Ara-CTP.
assumptions: Genetic variants related to metabolic enzymes influence the intracellular accumulation and retention of Ara-CTP, which affects the clinical outcome of cytarabine-based therapies.

gap_signals.text: The review highlights the need for more detailed and comprehensive information on the effects of Ara-C-based therapies, from biological to clinical practice, to maintain high interest among both researchers and clinical hematologists.

important_claims.claim: Individual variability in the clinical response to cytarabine-based treatments is associated with genetic variants related to metabolic enzymes.
important_claims.evidence: Several studies suggest that the individual variability in clinical response to leukemia and lymphoma treatments, underlying either Ara-C mechanism resistance or toxicity, appears to be associated with the intracellular accumulation and retention of Ara-CTP due to genetic variants related to metabolic enzymes.

causal_chains.trigger: Genetic variants related to metabolic enzymes

steps.claim: Influence intracellular accumulation and retention of Ara-CTP
steps.evidence: Several studies suggest that the individual variability in clinical response to leukemia and lymphoma treatments, underlying either Ara-C mechanism resistance or toxicity, appears to be associated with the intracellular accumulation and retention of Ara-CTP due to genetic variants related to metabolic enzymes.

steps.claim: Affect the clinical outcome of cytarabine-based therapies
steps.evidence: Ara-C-based chemotherapy regimens can induce a suboptimal clinical outcome in a fraction of patients.

causal_chains.outcome: Suboptimal clinical outcome in a fraction of patients treated with cytarabine
interventions: New drug formulations with optimized pharmacokinetics

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a foundation for understanding the role of pharmacogenomics in the efficacy and toxicity of cytarabine, which can guide future research into personalized medicine approaches for leukemia and lymphoma treatments. It also suggests areas for further investigation, such as the development of new drug formulations and the identification of additional biomarkers.

keynotes.title: The Role of Genetic Polymorphisms in High-Dose Methotrexate Toxicity and Response in Hematological Malignancies: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

keynote.title: The Role of Genetic Polymorphisms in High-Dose Methotrexate Toxicity and Response in Hematological Malignancies: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
keynote.core_problem: Interindividual variability in the pharmacokinetics and toxicity of high-dose methotrexate (HDMTX) due to genetic polymorphisms, complicating clinical utility.
contributions: Determined the role of gene variants within the HDMTX pathway and their association with clinical outcomes.
Suggested the potential utility of genotyping MTHFR and/or ABCB1 polymorphisms to tailor HDMTX therapy and reduce toxicity.
methods: Systematic review and meta-analysis following PRISMA guidelines.
Search conducted in EMBASE, PubMed, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL), and ClinicalTrials.gov from inception to November 2020.
Included studies on single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) within the HDMTX pathway, involving RFC1, SLCO1B1, ABCB1, FPGS, GGH, MTHFR, DHFR, TYMS, and ATIC.

Meta-analysis using Cochrane Collaboration Review Manager software 5.3 to evaluate associations between SNPs and clinical outcomes (odds ratios (ORs) or hazard ratios (HRs) with 95% confidence intervals).
experiments_or_evidence: 34 studies with 4102 subjects were identified for the association analysis.
Nine SNPs involving MTHFR, RFC1, ABCB1, SLCO1B1, TYMS, FPGS, and ATIC genes were investigated.

Two SNPs (MTHFR 677C>T and ABCB1 3435C>T) were statistically associated with increased risk of HDMTX toxicity, while three SNPs (TYMS 2R>3R, RFC1 80A>G, and MTHFR 1298A>C) showed a tendency toward decreased risk of HDMTX toxicity.
assumptions: Genetic variations play a significant role in the interindividual response to HDMTX.
limitations: Data on prognosis outcomes was substantially lacking, and current studies were underpowered to investigate the genetic association.

gap_signals.text: Data on prognosis outcomes was substantially lacking, and current studies were underpowered to investigate the genetic association.
the data of prognosis outcomes was substantially lacking, and current studies were underpowered to investigate the genetic association

datasets_or_materials: Studies from EMBASE, PubMed, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL), and ClinicalTrials.gov.

important_claims.claim: Genotyping of MTHFR and/or ABCB1 polymorphisms prior to treatment, particularly MTHFR 677C>T, is potentially useful for tailoring HDMTX therapy and reducing toxicity.
important_claims.evidence: Two SNPs (MTHFR 677C>T and ABCB1 3435C>T) were statistically associated with increased risk of HDMTX toxicity, while three SNPs (TYMS 2R>3R, RFC1 80A>G, and MTHFR 1298A>C) showed a tendency toward decreased risk of HDMTX toxicity.

causal_chains.trigger: Presence of specific SNPs (MTHFR 677C>T and ABCB1 3435C>T)

steps.claim: Increased risk of HDMTX toxicity (hepatotoxicity, mucositis, and renal toxicity)
steps.evidence: Statistically significant odds ratios (ORs) for MTHFR 677C>T and ABCB1 3435C>T with increased toxicity risks.

causal_chains.outcome: Increased risk of HDMTX toxicity
observables: Odds ratios (ORs) for hepatotoxicity, mucositis, and renal toxicity
interventions: Genotyping of MTHFR and/or ABCB1 polymorphisms before HDMTX treatment

causal_chains.trigger: Presence of specific SNPs (TYMS 2R>3R, RFC1 80A>G, and MTHFR 1298A>C)

steps.claim: Tendency toward decreased risk of HDMTX toxicity (mucositis, hepatotoxicity, and renal toxicity)
steps.evidence: Odds ratios (ORs) indicating a reduced risk for these SNPs.

causal_chains.outcome: Decreased risk of HDMTX toxicity
observables: Odds ratios (ORs) for mucositis, hepatotoxicity, and renal toxicity
interventions: Genotyping of TYMS 2R>3R, RFC1 80A>G, and MTHFR 1298A>C before HDMTX treatment

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This systematic review and meta-analysis provides a comprehensive overview of the genetic polymorphisms associated with HDMTX toxicity and response, which can be used as a foundation for future research to further validate the findings and explore additional genetic factors. The identified gaps in prognosis data also highlight the need for more studies to investigate the long-term outcomes and genetic associations in HDMTX therapy.

keynotes.title: The burden of rare variants in DPYS gene is a novel predictor of the risk of developing severe fluoropyrimidine-related toxicity

keynote.title: The burden of rare variants in DPYS gene is a novel predictor of the risk of developing severe fluoropyrimidine-related toxicity
keynote.core_problem: Rare genetic variants (minor allele frequency < 1%) in genes associated with drug adsorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination are poorly studied, and their impact on therapy outcomes, particularly in the context of fluoropyrimidine-related toxicity, is not well understood.
contributions: Identified that the rare mutational burden of DPYS is a significant predictor of severe fluoropyrimidine-related toxicity.
Found that some common genetic polymorphisms in DPYS and PPARD also have predictive value for severe toxicity.
methods: Next-generation sequencing of the coding sequence and untranslated regions of 54 genes related to fluoropyrimidine pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics in 120 patients with grade 3-5 toxicity and 104 matched controls.
Sequence Kernel Association Test (SKAT) analysis to select genes with a burden of genetic variants significantly associated with the risk of severe toxicity.
Statistical association of common and rare genetic variants in selected genes was further investigated.
Functional impact of genetic variants assessed using in silico prediction tools (Predict2SNP; ADME Prediction Framework).
experiments_or_evidence: SKAT analysis highlighted DPYS and PPARD as genes with a genetic mutational burden significantly associated with the risk of severe fluoropyrimidine-related toxicity (Bonferroni adjusted P = 0.024 and P = 0.039, respectively).
The burden of rare DPYS variants was significantly higher in patients with toxicity compared to controls (P = 0.047, Mann–Whitney test).
Carrying at least one rare DPYS variant was associated with an approximately fourfold higher risk of severe cumulative (OR = 4.08, P = 0.030) and acute (OR = 4.21, P = 0.082) toxicity.

Some common variants in DPYS and PPARD were identified: DPYS rs143004875-T and PPARD rs2016520-T variants predicted an increased risk of severe cumulative (P = 0.002 and P = 0.001, respectively) and acute (P = 0.005 and P = 0.0001, respectively) toxicity.
assumptions: Rare genetic variants can have a significant impact on the risk of severe toxicity from fluoropyrimidines.
Genes involved in the catabolic pathway of fluoropyrimidines, such as DPYS, are important for predicting toxicity.
limitations: The study cohort size is limited, and further validation in larger and more diverse populations is needed.

gap_signals.text: The study cohort size is limited, and further validation in larger and more diverse populations is needed.
Further validation in larger and more diverse populations is needed.

datasets_or_materials: Coding sequence and untranslated regions of 54 genes related to fluoropyrimidine pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics in 120 patients with grade 3-5 toxicity and 104 matched controls.

important_claims.claim: The rare mutational burden of DPYS is a promising pharmacogenetic marker for precision dosing of fluoropyrimidines.
important_claims.evidence: Carrying at least one rare DPYS variant was associated with an approximately fourfold higher risk of severe cumulative (OR = 4.08, P = 0.030) and acute (OR = 4.21, P = 0.082) toxicity.

important_claims.claim: Some common genetic polymorphisms in DPYS and PPARD are promising predictive markers for severe fluoropyrimidine-related toxicity.
important_claims.evidence: DPYS rs143004875-T and PPARD rs2016520-T variants predicted an increased risk of severe cumulative (P = 0.002 and P = 0.001, respectively) and acute (P = 0.005 and P = 0.0001, respectively) toxicity.

steps.claim: Higher burden of rare DPYS variants in patients with toxicity
steps.evidence: The burden of rare DPYS variants was significantly higher in patients with toxicity compared to controls (P = 0.047, Mann–Whitney test).

steps.claim: Increased risk of severe cumulative and acute toxicity
steps.evidence: Carrying at least one rare DPYS variant was associated with an approximately fourfold higher risk of severe cumulative (OR = 4.08, P = 0.030) and acute (OR = 4.21, P = 0.082) toxicity.

causal_chains.outcome: Increased risk of severe fluoropyrimidine-related toxicity
observables: Severity of cumulative and acute toxicity

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This work provides a foundation for further research into the role of rare genetic variants in predicting and managing severe fluoropyrimidine-related toxicity, potentially leading to more personalized and effective treatment strategies.

keynotes.title: Pharmacogenetics to optimize immunosuppressant therapy in systemic lupus erythematosus: a scoping review

keynote.title: Pharmacogenetics to optimize immunosuppressant therapy in systemic lupus erythematosus: a scoping review
keynote.core_problem: The influence of genetic polymorphisms on the pharmacokinetics and therapeutic responses of immunosuppressants in SLE is not well understood, especially in diverse populations, leading to suboptimal personalized treatment.
contributions: A comprehensive review of 37 studies focusing on the impact of genetic variants on the metabolism, efficacy, and toxicity of immunosuppressants used in SLE.
methods: Scoping review of 37 studies on the pharmacogenetics of immunosuppressants in SLE, including mycophenolic acid, tacrolimus, azathioprine, glucocorticoids, and cyclophosphamide.
experiments_or_evidence: Genetic variants in UGT1A9, UGT2B7, CYP3A5, ABCB1, ABCC2, and TPMT significantly affect drug metabolism, efficacy, and toxicity in SLE patients.
assumptions: Genetic polymorphisms play a significant role in the pharmacokinetics and therapeutic responses of immunosuppressants in SLE.
limitations: Most pharmacogenetic studies focus on organ transplantation, leaving a critical gap in SLE-specific research, particularly among diverse populations.

gap_signals.text: most pharmacogenetic studies focus on organ transplantation, leaving a critical gap in SLE-specific research, particularly among diverse populations

important_claims.claim: ABCB1 polymorphisms influence drug transport and bioavailability, impacting tacrolimus and glucocorticoid response.
important_claims.evidence: Reviewed studies indicate that ABCB1 polymorphisms affect the transport and bioavailability of drugs like tacrolimus and glucocorticoids.

important_claims.claim: ABCC2 variants alter MPA clearance, potentially affecting therapeutic outcomes.
important_claims.evidence: Reviewed studies show that ABCC2 variants can change the clearance of mycophenolic acid (MPA), which may impact the effectiveness of the treatment.

important_claims.claim: UGT1A9 and UGT2B7 variants influence mycophenolic acid metabolism.
important_claims.evidence: Studies reviewed suggest that UGT1A9 and UGT2B7 variants have a significant effect on the metabolism of mycophenolic acid.

important_claims.claim: CYP3A5 impacts tacrolimus dosing.
important_claims.evidence: Reviewed studies indicate that CYP3A5 polymorphisms influence the dosing requirements for tacrolimus.

important_claims.claim: TPMT determines azathioprine metabolism.
important_claims.evidence: Studies reviewed show that TPMT polymorphisms are crucial in determining the metabolism of azathioprine.

important_claims.claim: CYP2C19 and CYP2B6 affect cyclophosphamide processing.
important_claims.evidence: Reviewed studies indicate that CYP2C19 and CYP2B6 variants impact the processing of cyclophosphamide.

steps.evidence: Reviewed studies indicate that ABCB1 polymorphisms affect the transport and bioavailability of drugs like tacrolimus and glucocorticoids.

steps.evidence: Reviewed studies show that ABCC2 variants can change the clearance of mycophenolic acid (MPA).

steps.claim: UGT1A9 and UGT2B7 variants influence mycophenolic acid metabolism
steps.evidence: Studies reviewed suggest that UGT1A9 and UGT2B7 variants have a significant effect on the metabolism of mycophenolic acid.
