keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a foundation for future SLE-specific pharmacogenetic studies, highlighting the need for more research in diverse populations. It also underscores the potential for integrating pharmacogenetics into clinical practice to enhance the safety, efficacy, and outcomes of immunosuppressive therapy in SLE.

keynotes.title: UGT1A1 gene variants associated with irinotecan toxicity in patients from northeastern Mexico

keynote.title: UGT1A1 gene variants associated with irinotecan toxicity in patients from northeastern Mexico
keynote.core_problem: Limited data on the prevalence of clinically relevant UGT1A1 variants and their association with Irinotecan toxicity in Mexican populations, particularly in Northeastern Mexico.
contributions: Characterized the pharmacogenetic profile of UGT1A1 in Northeastern Mexico
Identified a high prevalence of UGT1A1 variants associated with Irinotecan toxicity
Highlighted the need for population-specific pre-treatment UGT1A1 genotyping and regional dosing guidelines
methods: Observational case–control study with ongoing enrollment
Cases: patients with gastrointestinal malignancies treated with Irinotecan-based chemotherapy
Controls: cancer-free individuals from the general population with no prior exposure to Irinotecan
UGT1A1 genotyping for alleles *28, *36, and *37 by Sanger sequencing
Allele *6 analyzed using a qPCR-based assay
Toxicities graded according to CTCAE criteria
experiments_or_evidence: Interim evaluation of 211 individuals (11 cases and 200 controls)
UGT1A1*28 was the most frequent variant (allele frequency: 0.30)
Rare alleles UGT1A1*36 (frequency: 0.0075) and UGT1A1*37 (frequency: 0.0025) identified
No UGT1A1*6 alleles detected
Genotype-predicted phenotypes: 8.5% poor metabolizers, 43% intermediate metabolizers, 1.5% ultrarapid metabolizers
27.3% of Irinotecan-treated patients developed grade ≥3 treatment-related toxicity
Common adverse events: gastrointestinal and hematologic, predominantly grade 1–2
Grade ≥3 toxicities: nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, and fatigue (each 9.1%)
Carriers of UGT1A1 toxicity-associated variants showed a higher incidence of severe toxicity compared with non-carriers (37.5% vs 0%)
assumptions: Genetic variants in the UGT1A1 gene affect Irinotecan metabolism and are associated with an increased risk of treatment-related toxicity.
limitations: Limited sample size
Exploratory association between UGT1A1 variants and severe toxicity

gap_signals.text: Exploratory association between UGT1A1 variants and severe toxicity
Limited sample size
Data on the prevalence of clinically relevant UGT1A1 variants and their association with Irinotecan toxicity in Mexican populations are limited.

datasets_or_materials: Sanger sequencing for UGT1A1 alleles *28, *36, and *37
qPCR-based assay for UGT1A1 allele *6
CTCAE criteria for grading toxicities

important_claims.claim: The mestizo population of northeastern Mexico presents a distinctive UGT1A1 allele frequency profile characterized by a high prevalence of variants associated with Irinotecan toxicity, differing from those reported in other Latin American populations.
important_claims.evidence: UGT1A1*28 was the most frequent variant (allele frequency: 0.30). The rare alleles UGT1A1*36 (frequency: 0.0075) and UGT1A1*37 (frequency: 0.0025) were identified, representing the first report of these variants in the Mexican mestizo population.

important_claims.claim: These findings support the relevance of population-specific pre-treatment UGT1A1 genotyping and highlight the need for regional dosing guidelines to reduce severe toxicity and optimize Irinotecan therapy in underrepresented populations.
important_claims.evidence: Carriers of UGT1A1 toxicity-associated variants showed a higher incidence of severe toxicity compared with non-carriers (37.5% vs 0%).

steps.claim: Individuals carrying UGT1A1 toxicity-associated variants have a higher incidence of severe toxicity
steps.evidence: Carriers of UGT1A1 toxicity-associated variants showed a higher incidence of severe toxicity compared with non-carriers (37.5% vs 0%).

causal_chains.outcome: Higher incidence of severe toxicity in carriers of UGT1A1 toxicity-associated variants

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The study provides valuable insights into the pharmacogenetic profile of UGT1A1 in a specific population, which can be used to inform future research on personalized medicine and the development of regional dosing guidelines for Irinotecan therapy.

keynotes.title: Genetic Polymorphisms Associated with Vincristine Pharmacokinetics and Vincristine-Induced Peripheral Neuropathy in Pediatric Oncology Patients

keynote.title: Genetic Polymorphisms Associated with Vincristine Pharmacokinetics and Vincristine-Induced Peripheral Neuropathy in Pediatric Oncology Patients
keynote.core_problem: Vincristine (VCR) is a key component of chemotherapy for childhood cancers, but its main side effect, VCR-induced peripheral neuropathy (VIPN), limits its use. Some children are more susceptible to VIPN, which is at least partially dependent on genetic factors and pharmacokinetics (PK).
contributions: Identification and replication of genetic variants (SNPs) associated with VCR pharmacokinetics and VIPN.
Potential for individualizing VCR treatment based on genetic susceptibility.
methods: Patient samples from a randomized clinical trial studying the effect of administration duration of VCR on VIPN in 90 patients were used.
PK sampling was conducted on between one and five occasions at multiple time points.
A linear two-compartment model with first-order elimination was used, and targeted next-generation DNA sequencing was performed.

Genotype–trait associations were analyzed using mixed-effect models or logistic regression analysis for repeated measures, or Poisson regression analysis in which the highest VIPN score per patient was included.
experiments_or_evidence: Nine single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in seven genes (NDRG1, GARS, FIG4, FGD4, SEPTIN9, CEP72, and ETAA1) were associated with VIPN.
Three SNPs in three genes (MTNR1B, RAB7A, and SNU13) were associated with PK of VCR.
assumptions: The study assumes that genetic factors and pharmacokinetics play a significant role in the development of VIPN.
limitations: The study needs further replication to confirm the findings.

gap_signals.text: The study needs further replication to confirm the findings.
It is important that future studies try to replicate these findings.

datasets_or_materials: Patient samples from a randomized clinical trial involving 90 patients.

important_claims.claim: PK of VCR and VIPN are influenced by SNPs.
important_claims.evidence: Nine SNPs in seven genes were associated with VIPN, and three SNPs in three genes were associated with PK of VCR.

important_claims.claim: Upfront identification of SNPs could help individualize VCR treatment.
important_claims.evidence: The study suggests that identifying genetic variants that lead to altered susceptibility to VIPN or VCR exposure can help in personalizing treatment.

steps.claim: SNPs in NDRG1, GARS, FIG4, FGD4, SEPTIN9, CEP72, and ETAA1 are associated with VIPN.
steps.evidence: Nine SNPs in seven genes (NDRG1, GARS, FIG4, FGD4, SEPTIN9, CEP72, and ETAA1) were associated with VIPN.

steps.claim: SNPs in MTNR1B, RAB7A, and SNU13 are associated with VCR PK.
steps.evidence: Three SNPs in three genes (MTNR1B, RAB7A, and SNU13) were associated with PK of VCR.

causal_chains.outcome: Altered susceptibility to VIPN or VCR exposure
interventions: Mixed-effect models or logistic regression analysis for repeated measures

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The identified SNPs and their associations with VCR PK and VIPN provide a foundation for further research into personalized VCR treatment, potentially leading to improved outcomes and reduced toxicity in pediatric oncology patients.

keynotes.title: Impact of Genetic Variants on Pregabalin Pharmacokinetics and Safety

keynote.title: Impact of Genetic Variants on Pregabalin Pharmacokinetics and Safety
keynote.core_problem: Understanding the impact of genetic variants, particularly in transporters and metabolic enzymes, on the pharmacokinetics and safety of pregabalin.
contributions: Identified that NAT2 slow acetylators (SAs) have a 16-18% increase in exposure to pregabalin compared to intermediate and normal acetylators.
Found that sex and sex-related exposure differences are the main predictors of adverse drug reactions (ADRs) with pregabalin.
methods: Conducted a bioequivalence clinical trial with 24 healthy volunteers, administering pregabalin 300 mg orally.
Genotyped 23 subjects for 114 variants in 31 candidate genes to explore their impact on pregabalin pharmacokinetics and safety.
experiments_or_evidence: Measured uncorrected mean AUC∞ and Cmax, which were significantly higher in females than in males (p = 0.002 and p = 0.001, respectively).
Observed no differences in dose/weight (DW)-corrected exposure metrics between sexes.
assumptions: Assumed that genetic variants in certain genes encoding for transporters and metabolic enzymes could influence the efficacy and safety of pregabalin.
limitations: The study was conducted with a small sample size (24 healthy volunteers).
The study focused on a limited number of genetic variants and did not account for all possible genetic factors.

gap_signals.text: The study focused on a limited number of genetic variants and did not account for all possible genetic factors.
The study was conducted with a small sample size (24 healthy volunteers).
Further studies with larger and more diverse populations are needed to confirm the findings and explore other potential genetic factors.

datasets_or_materials: Clinical trial data from 24 healthy volunteers.

important_claims.claim: NAT2 slow acetylators (SAs) showed a 16–18% increase in exposure compared to intermediate (IAs) and normal (NAs) acetylators.
important_claims.evidence: NAT2 SAs exhibited a 25% higher t1/2 as compared with NAT2 IAs and 58% higher compared to NAT2 NAs.

important_claims.claim: Sex and sex-related exposure differences are the main predictors of ADR occurrence.
important_claims.evidence: Females had significantly higher AUC∞ and Cmax values compared to males (p = 0.002 and p = 0.001, respectively), and these differences were the main predictors of ADRs.

steps.claim: NAT2 slow acetylators (SAs) show a 16–18% increase in exposure to pregabalin.
steps.evidence: NAT2 SAs exhibited a 25% higher t1/2 as compared with NAT2 IAs and 58% higher compared to NAT2 NAs.

causal_chains.outcome: Increased exposure to pregabalin

steps.claim: Females have significantly higher AUC∞ and Cmax values compared to males.
steps.evidence: The uncorrected mean (±SD) of AUC∞ and Cmax were 61,097 ± 14,762 ng*h/mL and 7802 ± 1659 ng/mL, respectively, which were significantly higher in females than in males (p = 0.002 and p = 0.001, respectively).

steps.claim: Higher exposure in females is associated with a higher incidence of ADRs.
steps.evidence: Sex and sex-related exposure differences (i.e., females and their higher exposure compared to males) were the main predictors of ADR occurrence.

causal_chains.outcome: Higher incidence of ADRs in females
observables: AUC∞ and Cmax values, ADR occurrence

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides foundational data on the role of genetic variants, particularly NAT2, in the pharmacokinetics and safety of pregabalin, which can be used to design more targeted and personalized treatment strategies. The findings also highlight the importance of considering sex and sex-related exposure differences in the evaluation of drug safety.

keynotes.title: Prevalence of risk phenotypes associated with CYP2C9*2, *3, and VKORC1 c.-1639G>A genetic polymorphisms in world populations: implications in clinical practice

keynote.title: Prevalence of risk phenotypes associated with CYP2C9*2, *3, and VKORC1 c.-1639G>A genetic polymorphisms in world populations: implications in clinical practice
keynote.core_problem: The safety or efficacy of drugs may be affected by the genetic variability of CYP2C9 or VKORC1, leading to increased risk of drug-related toxicities such as bleeding events.
contributions: Identification of the prevalence of risk phenotypes associated with CYP2C9*2, *3, and VKORC1 c.-1639G>A genetic polymorphisms in different ethnic groups
Highlighting the need for pharmacogenomics-based dosing guidelines
methods: Obtained allele frequencies of CYP2C9*2, *3, and VKORC1 c.-1639G>A from the 1000 Genomes Project Phase III
Assigned predictive risk phenotypes and correlations based on carrying characteristic allele carriers following international PGx-based dosing guidelines
experiments_or_evidence: Predicted intermediate and poor metabolizers (17.8%; 95% CI 16.3%–19.3%) due to inheriting CYP2C9*2 and *3 genetic variants

Highest risk phenotypes were observed in European (35%; 95% CI 30.8%–39.2%), followed by South Asian (26.8%; 95% CI 22.9%–30.7%), American (25.9%; 95% CI 21.3%–30.5%), East Asian (6.7%; 95% CI 4.5%–8.9%), and African populations (2.1%; 95% CI 1%–3.2%)

Sensitive and highly sensitive responders (33.1%; 95% CI 31.3%–35%) when combining CYP2C9*2 and *3 variants with VKORC1c.-1639G>A, most prevalent in East Asian (79.6%; 95% CI 76%–83.1%), followed by European (38.6%; 95% CI 34.3%–42.8%), American (30%; 95% CI 25.2%–34.8%), South Asian (25.2%; 95% CI...[truncated]
Statistically significant prevalence of risk phenotypes in different ethnic groups (p < 0.05; 1.94 × 10−175, χ 2 test)
assumptions: The genetic variability of CYP2C9 or VKORC1 can affect the safety or efficacy of drugs
limitations: Further studies are needed to evaluate the clinical benefits of integrating PGx-based therapies in routine practice

gap_signals.text: further studies are needed to evaluate the clinical benefits of integrating PGx-based therapies in routine practice

datasets_or_materials: 1000 Genomes Project Phase III data

important_claims.claim: Approximately one-fifth of the global population is at risk based on the CYP2C9*2 and *3 genotypes
important_claims.evidence: Intermediate and poor metabolizers were predicted to be risk phenotypes (17.8%; 95% CI 16.3%–19.3%) due to inheriting CYP2C9*2 and *3 genetic variants.

important_claims.claim: Approximately one-third of the population is at risk when considering the combination of CYP2C9 and VKORC1 c.-1639G>A genotypes
important_claims.evidence: Sensitive and highly sensitive responders were considered risk phenotypes (33.1%; 95% CI 31.3%–35%) when combining CYP2C9*2 and *3 variants with VKORC1c.-1639G>A.

causal_chains.trigger: Presence of CYP2C9*2, *3, and VKORC1 c.-1639G>A genetic polymorphisms

steps.claim: Individuals with these genetic variants are classified as intermediate or poor metabolizers
steps.evidence: Intermediate and poor metabolizers were predicted to be risk phenotypes (17.8%; 95% CI 16.3%–19.3%) due to inheriting CYP2C9*2 and *3 genetic variants.

steps.claim: These individuals are at increased risk of drug-related toxicities, such as bleeding events
steps.evidence: Patients may be at increased risk of drug-related toxicities, for example, bleeding events, if they carry CYP2C9*2 (rs1799853), CYP2C9*3 (rs1057910), or VKORC1 c.-1639G>A (rs9923231) genetic variants.

causal_chains.outcome: Increased risk of adverse drug reactions
observables: Drug-related toxicities, such as bleeding events

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides a foundation for understanding the prevalence of risk phenotypes across different ethnic groups, which can inform the development and implementation of personalized medicine strategies, particularly in the context of pharmacogenomics-based dosing guidelines.

keynotes.title: Association Study Among Candidate Genetic Polymorphisms and Chemotherapy-Related Severe Toxicity in Testicular Cancer Patients

keynote.title: Association Study Among Candidate Genetic Polymorphisms and Chemotherapy-Related Severe Toxicity in Testicular Cancer Patients
keynote.core_problem: The adverse effects of chemotherapy, specifically BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin), vary significantly among individuals with testicular cancer, leading to severe toxicity. The study aims to explore the association between genetic polymorphisms and these toxicities.
contributions: Identified specific genetic polymorphisms associated with severe adverse drug reactions (ADRs) in testicular cancer patients treated with BEP chemotherapy.
Provided evidence for the use of pharmacogenetics in predicting severe ADRs.
methods: Prospectively recruited 119 testicular cancer patients from 2010 to 2017.
Analyzed genetic polymorphisms using PCR and/or qPCR with TaqMan® probes.
Evaluated toxicity based on the Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events, v4.03.
Performed univariate and multivariate logistic regression analyses to identify significant associations.
experiments_or_evidence: Univariate statistical analysis revealed that certain genotypes (e.g., ERCC2 rs13181 T/G, CYP3A4 rs2740574 A/G, ERCC2 rs238406 C/C, GSTT1-null, ERCC2 rs1799793 A/A, BLMH rs1050565 G/G, GSTP1 G/G) are associated with specific severe ADRs (alopecia, leukopenia, lymphocytopenia, anemia, pain, infection...[truncated]
Multivariate analysis showed an association between specific ERCC1/2 genotypes and cumulative dose of BEP drugs with the appearance of severe leukopenia and/or febrile neutropenia.
Grades III–IV vomiting, nausea, and alopecia could be partly explained by the presence of specific ERCC1/2, MDR1, GSTP1, and BLMH genotypes (p < 0.05).
assumptions: Genetic polymorphisms in genes related to the pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics of BEP and DNA repair enzymes may influence the severity of chemotherapy-induced toxicity.
limitations: The study is limited to a specific population (Chilean testicular cancer patients) and may not generalize to other populations.
The sample size is relatively small (119 patients), which may limit the power of the study.

gap_signals.text: The sample size is relatively small (119 patients), which may limit the power of the study.
The study is limited to a specific population (Chilean testicular cancer patients) and may not generalize to other populations.
The study provides evidence for the usefulness of pharmacogenetics, but further validation in larger and more diverse populations is needed.

datasets_or_materials: 119 testicular cancer patients diagnosed from 2010 to 2017.

important_claims.claim: Specific genetic polymorphisms are associated with severe ADRs in testicular cancer patients treated with BEP chemotherapy.
important_claims.evidence: Univariate and multivariate logistic regression analyses identified significant associations between certain genotypes and severe ADRs (p < 0.05).

important_claims.claim: Pharmacogenetics can be used as a tool for predicting severe ADRs in testicular cancer patients treated with BEP chemotherapy.
important_claims.evidence: The study provided evidence for the association between specific genotypes and severe ADRs, suggesting the potential for pharmacogenetic testing to predict and manage these toxicities.

causal_chains.trigger: Presence of specific genetic polymorphisms (e.g., ERCC2, CYP3A4, GSTT1, BLMH, GSTP1)

steps.claim: Patients with specific genotypes (e.g., ERCC2 rs13181 T/G, CYP3A4 rs2740574 A/G, ERCC2 rs238406 C/C, GSTT1-null, ERCC2 rs1799793 A/A, BLMH rs1050565 G/G, GSTP1 G/G) are more likely to develop severe ADRs.
steps.evidence: Univariate statistical analyses revealed that patients with these genotypes are more likely to develop specific severe ADRs (p < 0.05).

causal_chains.outcome: Development of severe ADRs (e.g., alopecia, leukopenia, lymphocytopenia, anemia, pain, infection).
observables: Severe ADRs (III–IV) such as alopecia, leukopenia, lymphocytopenia, anemia, pain, and infection.
interventions: Genotyping of patients before BEP chemotherapy to predict and manage severe ADRs.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The study provides a foundation for further research into the role of pharmacogenetics in predicting and managing severe ADRs in testicular cancer patients. It highlights the importance of considering genetic factors in personalized medicine approaches to chemotherapy.

keynotes.title: Small Cell Lung Cancer and Tarlatamab: A Meta-Analysis of Clinical Trials

keynote.title: Small Cell Lung Cancer and Tarlatamab: A Meta-Analysis of Clinical Trials
keynote.core_problem: Evaluating the efficacy and safety of tarlatamab, a DLL3-directed bispecific T-cell engager, as monotherapy and in combination regimens for the treatment of advanced small cell lung cancer (SCLC).
contributions: Systematic review and meta-analysis of clinical trials evaluating tarlatamab in SCLC.
Pooled analysis of objective response rate (ORR) and disease control rate (DCR).
Assessment of survival outcomes using Kaplan–Meier methods.
methods: Conducted in accordance with PRISMA 2020 guidelines.
PubMed/MEDLINE and EMBASE searched from inception through September 2025.
Eligible studies reported quantifiable efficacy and/or safety outcomes.
Random-effects models used to pool ORR and DCR.
Kaplan–Meier methods applied to assess survival outcomes.
experiments_or_evidence: Seven clinical trials involving 1,247 patients with advanced SCLC were included.
Pooled ORR was 0.42 (95% CI 0.31–0.54), with response rates ranging from 21–47% in monotherapy and up to 48% in combination regimens.
Pooled DCR was 0.48 (95% CI 0.31–0.66), with DCR reaching up to 87% in combination settings.
Median progression-free survival ranged from 3.5 to 5.6 months, and median overall survival ranged from 13.2 to 25.3 months.
Grade 3 and grade 4 adverse events occurred in 5.4% and 1.4% of patients, respectively.
assumptions: The studies included are representative of the broader population of SCLC patients.
The random-effects model is appropriate for pooling the data.
limitations: Safety reporting was incomplete in several studies.
The meta-analysis includes early-phase trials, which may not fully represent the long-term effects and safety profile of tarlatamab.

gap_signals.text: Safety reporting was incomplete in several studies.
The meta-analysis includes early-phase trials, which may not fully represent the long-term effects and safety profile of tarlatamab.
Further prospective studies are needed to define the optimal role of tarlatamab in the evolving SCLC treatment landscape.

datasets_or_materials: Clinical trial data from seven studies involving 1,247 patients with advanced SCLC.

important_claims.claim: Tarlatamab demonstrates clinically meaningful antitumor activity with an acceptable safety profile in heavily pretreated SCLC.
important_claims.evidence: Pooled ORR was 0.42 (95% CI 0.31–0.54), with response rates ranging from 21–47% in monotherapy and up to 48% in combination regimens. Pooled DCR was 0.48 (95% CI 0.31–0.66), with DCR reaching up to 87% in combination settings. Grade 3 and grade 4 adverse events occurred in 5.4% and 1.4% of patients, respectively.

causal_chains.trigger: Use of tarlatamab in SCLC patients

steps.evidence: Pooled ORR was 0.42 (95% CI 0.31–0.54), with response rates ranging from 21–47% in monotherapy and up to 48% in combination regimens.

steps.claim: Pooled DCR was 0.48 (95% CI 0.31–0.66)
steps.evidence: Pooled DCR was 0.48 (95% CI 0.31–0.66), with DCR reaching up to 87% in combination settings.

steps.claim: Median progression-free survival ranged from 3.5 to 5.6 months, and median overall survival ranged from 13.2 to 25.3 months
steps.evidence: Median progression-free survival ranged from 3.5 to 5.6 months, while median overall survival ranged from 13.2 to 25.3 months.

causal_chains.outcome: Clinically meaningful antitumor activity with an acceptable safety profile

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The meta-analysis provides a comprehensive evaluation of the efficacy and safety of tarlatamab in SCLC, which can be used to inform future clinical trials and guide the development of DLL3-targeted therapies.

keynotes.title: Prospective Genotyping of Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Clinical Implications of Next-Generation Sequencing for Matching Patients to Targeted and Immune Therapies

keynote.title: Prospective Genotyping of Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Clinical Implications of Next-Generation Sequencing for Matching Patients to Targeted and Immune Therapies
keynote.core_problem: Determining the clinical utility of next-generation sequencing (NGS) in providing predictive and prognostic information for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) patients, guiding therapeutic decision-making, and matching patients to appropriate targeted and immune therapies.
contributions: Demonstrated that NGS can identify actionable genetic alterations in HCC patients, which may influence treatment outcomes and response to therapy.
methods: Matched tumor/normal DNA from 127 HCC patients were analyzed using a hybridization capture–based NGS assay targeting 341 or more cancer-associated genes. Demographic and treatment data were prospectively collected to correlate molecular profiles with treatment outcomes and drug responses.
experiments_or_evidence: WNT/β-catenin pathway and TP53 alterations were found to be frequent and mutually exclusive in HCC patients. PI3K–mTOR pathway alterations in sorafenib-treated patients were associated with worse disease control rates, shorter progression-free survival (PFS), and overall survival (OS). WNT/β-catenin...[truncated]
assumptions: The presence of specific genetic alterations (e.g., WNT/β-catenin, TP53, PI3K–mTOR) influences the response to systemic therapies and can be used to guide therapeutic decisions.
limitations: The study is limited by its sample size, and the findings need further validation in larger cohorts. The impact of NGS on long-term patient outcomes was not fully explored.

gap_signals.text: The study is limited by its sample size, and the findings need further validation in larger cohorts. The impact of NGS on long-term patient outcomes was not fully explored.
The long-term impact of NGS on patient outcomes was not fully explored, suggesting a need for follow-up studies.
The study's findings need further validation in larger cohorts to confirm the generalizability of the results.

important_claims.evidence: WNT/β-catenin pathway (45%) and TP53 (33%) alterations were frequent and represented mutually exclusive molecular subsets.

important_claims.claim: PI3K–mTOR pathway alterations in sorafenib-treated patients are associated with worse treatment outcomes.
important_claims.evidence: In sorafenib-treated patients (n = 81), oncogenic PI3K–mTOR pathway alterations were associated with lower disease control rates, shorter median PFS, and shorter median OS.

important_claims.claim: WNT/β-catenin signaling alterations in patients treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors are associated with worse treatment outcomes.

keynotes.title: Targeted therapies for KRAS-mutant non-small cell lung cancer: from preclinical studies to clinical development—a narrative review

keynote.title: Targeted therapies for KRAS-mutant non-small cell lung cancer: from preclinical studies to clinical development—a narrative review
keynote.core_problem: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) with KRAS driver alterations has a poor prognosis with standard therapies, and there is a need for effective targeted treatments.
contributions: Review of the biology of KRAS-mutant tumors and data from preclinical and clinical trials on KRAS-targeted therapies in NSCLC patients with KRAS G12C mutation.
methods: Narrative review of literature on KRAS and its mutations, focusing on KRAS G12C inhibitors.
experiments_or_evidence: Clinical trial data demonstrating the efficacy and safety of Sotorasib and Adagrasib in pretreated NSCLC patients with KRAS G12C mutation.
limitations: Mechanisms of intrinsic and acquired resistance limit the activity of these agents.

gap_signals.text: Mechanisms of intrinsic and acquired resistance limit the activity of these agents.

Various studies testing KRAS inhibitors in different settings of disease, as single-agent or in combination with targeted agents for synthetic lethality and immunotherapy, are currently ongoing to further improve clinical outcomes.

important_claims.claim: Sotorasib is the first selective KRAS G12C inhibitor to receive approval based on significant clinical benefit and tolerable safety profile in previously treated, KRAS G12C-mutated NSCLC.
important_claims.evidence: Clinical trial data

important_claims.claim: Adagrasib, a highly selective covalent inhibitor of KRAS G12C, has also shown efficacy in pretreated patients.
important_claims.evidence: Clinical trial data

causal_chains.trigger: Discovery of selective KRAS G12C inhibitors

steps.claim: Selective KRAS G12C inhibitors provide significant clinical benefit in pretreated NSCLC patients with KRAS G12C mutation.
steps.evidence: Clinical trial data showing efficacy of Sotorasib and Adagrasib

causal_chains.outcome: Changed therapeutic scenario for KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC
observables: Improved clinical outcomes in pretreated patients
interventions: Use of Sotorasib and Adagrasib in clinical practice

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The review provides a comprehensive overview of the current state of KRAS G12C inhibitors, highlighting their clinical benefits and the challenges posed by resistance mechanisms. It serves as a valuable resource for researchers and clinicians working on improving treatment options for KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC.

keynotes.title: Society for Immunotherapy of Cancer (SITC) consensus definitions for resistance to combinations of immune checkpoint inhibitors with targeted therapies

keynote.title: Society for Immunotherapy of Cancer (SITC) consensus definitions for resistance to combinations of immune checkpoint inhibitors with targeted therapies
keynote.core_problem: Many tumors do not respond to treatment with single-agent immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs), and even when combined with targeted therapies, many patients still progress after combination treatment, indicating a need for new strategies to address resistance to immunotherapy.
contributions: Development of consensus clinical definitions for resistance to combinations of anti-PD-(L)1 ICIs and targeted therapies.
assumptions: Combination strategies with ICIs can enhance antitumor activity compared to single-agent treatments.
limitations: The paper focuses on the development of definitions and does not provide experimental or observational data.

gap_signals.text: The paper focuses on the development of definitions and does not provide experimental or observational data.

Despite improved survival and response rates for some patients when antiangiogenic or targeted therapies are administered with ICIs, many patients continue to progress after combination treatment and urgently need new strategies to address this manifestation of resistance to immunotherapy.

important_claims.claim: Combination strategies with ICIs have demonstrated enhanced antitumor activity compared with tumor-targeted or antiangiogenic therapy alone in randomized trials in a variety of solid tumor settings.
important_claims.evidence: Leading to regulatory approval from the US Food and Drug Administration and agencies in other countries for the treatment of endometrial cancer, kidney cancer, melanoma, and hepatocellular carcinoma.

important_claims.claim: Many patients continue to progress after combination treatment, indicating a need for new strategies to address resistance to immunotherapy.
important_claims.evidence: Despite improved survival and response rates for some patients when antiangiogenic or targeted therapies are administered with ICIs, many patients continue to progress after combination treatment and urgently need new strategies to address this manifestation of resistance to immunotherapy.

steps.claim: Combination strategies with ICIs demonstrate enhanced antitumor activity compared to single-agent treatments.
steps.evidence: Combination strategies with ICIs have demonstrated enhanced antitumor activity compared with tumor-targeted or antiangiogenic therapy alone in randomized trials in a variety of solid tumor settings, leading to regulatory approval from the US Food and Drug Administration and agencies in other countries for the treatment of endometrial cancer, kidney cancer, melanoma, and hepatocellular carcinoma.

causal_chains.outcome: Improved survival and response rates for some patients, but many still progress, indicating a need for new strategies to address resistance.
observables: Improved survival and response rates for some patients
Many patients still progress after combination treatment
interventions: Combination of ICIs with targeted therapies

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The consensus definitions provided in this paper can be used as a standardized framework for future research and clinical trial design, particularly in the context of understanding and addressing resistance to ICI-based combination therapies.

keynotes.title: Abstract B044: Deep learning-based integration of tumor omics and functional drug screening for precision treatment selection in high-risk and metastatic breast tumors

keynote.title: Deep learning-based integration of tumor omics and functional drug screening for precision treatment selection in high-risk and metastatic breast tumors
keynote.core_problem: The lack of actionable molecular alterations and poor predictive power of individual biomarkers in metastatic breast tumors, leading to the continued use of cytotoxic chemotherapy without data-driven tools for optimal selection.
contributions: A unified computational/experimental approach combining tumor omic and functional profiling with a deep learning (DL)-based framework (ScreenDL) for personalized treatment recommendations.
ScreenDL outperforms existing DL models in predicting drug response in patient-derived xenograft (PDX)-derived organoid (PDxO) models.
methods: General-purpose pretraining on large-scale cell line pharmaco-omic screens.
Fine-tuning on a newly generated pharmaco-omic dataset spanning >100 PDxO models from high-risk, endocrine-resistant, treatment-refractory, and/or metastatic breast tumors.
Patient-specific fine-tuning using ScreenAhead, integrating tumor transcriptomic and functional characteristics with prior knowledge.
experiments_or_evidence: Baseline performance: ScreenDL achieved a median Pearson correlation (PCC) of 0.32 between observed and predicted drug response, compared to 0.20 for the next-best tested model.
After PDxO-specific fine-tuning with ScreenAhead, ScreenDL provided high-confidence predictions (PCC > 0.5) for 49 out of 95 drugs, with a median PCC of 0.59 across all 95 drugs.
Retrospective preclinical pilot of 11 PDX models resulted in a 100% clinical benefit rate and a 65% objective response rate.
assumptions: Tumor omic and functional profiling can be effectively combined with deep learning to predict drug response.
Pretraining on large-scale cell line data and fine-tuning on patient-derived models can improve the accuracy of drug response predictions.
limitations: The study is based on a limited number of PDX models and may not fully represent the diversity of high-risk and metastatic breast tumors.
The retrospective nature of the preclinical pilot may not fully capture the real-world variability and complexity of patient responses.

gap_signals.text: The retrospective nature of the preclinical pilot may not fully capture the real-world variability and complexity of patient responses.
The study is based on a limited number of PDX models and may not fully represent the diversity of high-risk and metastatic breast tumors.
The potential for clinical translation and the need for prospective clinical trials to evaluate the effectiveness of the approach in real-world settings.
The need for further validation in a larger and more diverse set of patient samples to confirm the robustness of the model.

datasets_or_materials: Large-scale cell line pharmaco-omic screens.
Pharmaco-omic dataset spanning >100 PDxO models from high-risk, endocrine-resistant, treatment-refractory, and/or metastatic breast tumors.
code_or_implementation: ScreenDL: A deep learning-based cancer drug response prediction framework.

important_claims.claim: ScreenDL outperforms existing DL models in predicting drug response in PDxOs.
important_claims.evidence: At baseline, ScreenDL achieved a median Pearson correlation (PCC) of 0.32 between observed and predicted response, compared to 0.20 for the next-best tested model.

important_claims.claim: Personalized fine-tuning with ScreenAhead improves prediction accuracy.
important_claims.evidence: After incorporating each PDxO’s response to just 12 drugs through PDxO-specific fine-tuning with ScreenAhead, ScreenDL provided high-confidence predictions (PCC > 0.5) for 49 out of 95 drugs, with a median PCC of 0.59 across all 95 drugs.

causal_chains.trigger: Combining tumor omic and functional profiling with deep learning

steps.claim: ScreenDL is pretrained on large-scale cell line pharmaco-omic screens.
steps.evidence: General-purpose pretraining on large-scale cell line pharmaco-omic screens.

steps.claim: ScreenDL is fine-tuned on a dataset of >100 PDxO models.
steps.evidence: Follow-up fine-tuning on a newly generated pharmaco-omic dataset spanning >100 PDxO models primarily from high-risk, endocrine-resistant, treatment-refractory, and/or metastatic breast tumors.

steps.claim: ScreenAhead module integrates tumor transcriptomic and functional characteristics for patient-specific fine-tuning.
steps.evidence: As new patients enter our precision oncology pipeline, tumor omic profiling and functional drug screening in PDOs enables patient-specific fine-tuning with our ScreenAhead module.

causal_chains.outcome: Improved drug response predictions for high-risk and metastatic breast tumors.
observables: Median Pearson correlation (PCC) between observed and predicted drug response.
High-confidence predictions (PCC > 0.5) for a subset of drugs.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The unified computational/experimental approach and the ScreenDL framework can be adapted and applied to other types of cancer and therapeutic areas, providing a robust method for personalized treatment selection.

keynotes.title: PRR22: A Novel Prognostic Indicator and Therapeutic Target for Prostate Cancer.

keynote.title: PRR22: A Novel Prognostic Indicator and Therapeutic Target for Prostate Cancer
keynote.core_problem: Prostate cancer (PRAD) has limited prognostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets, and the role of PRR22 in PRAD remains undefined.
contributions: Systematically investigated the clinical relevance and mechanistic implications of PRR22 in PRAD.
Identified PRR22 as a novel, independent prognostic biomarker and therapeutic target in PRAD.
methods: Analyzed PRR22 expression in TCGA-PRAD, GSE55945, and Human Protein Atlas datasets.
Assessed prognostic value via Kaplan-Meier and multivariate Cox analyses.
Derived mechanistic insights from GSEA, immune infiltration profiling, MSI/mRNA-si correlations, and drug sensitivity analysis.
Performed experimental validation via qRT-PCR in PRAD cell lines.
experiments_or_evidence: PRR22 was significantly upregulated in PRAD tissues compared to normal tissues (p < 0.001).
PRR22 independently predicted shorter progression-free survival (HR = 1.82, p = 0.009).
Novel associations were identified between PRR22 and TGF-β signaling, immune evasion, microsatellite instability, and stemness.
High PRR22 correlated with resistance to multiple drugs (e.g., bicalutamide, vorinostat).
limitations: The study requires further clinical validation.
The molecular mechanisms underlying PRR22's role in PRAD need further exploration.

keynotes.title: Development of a mouse model of Pemphigus Vulgaris as a tool to evaluate nanoparticle induced antigen-specific tolerance

keynote.title: Development of a mouse model of Pemphigus Vulgaris as a tool to evaluate nanoparticle induced antigen-specific tolerance
keynote.core_problem: Pemphigus vulgaris (PV) is an autoimmune blistering disease caused by autoantibodies mainly directed against desmoglein (Dsg) 3. Current treatments are limited to broad systemic immunosuppression and B-cell depletion, which are not specific and can have significant side effects.
contributions: Developed a preclinical mouse model of PV using HLA-transgenic mice to study Dsg3-specific CD4+ T cell directed therapy.
Demonstrated the induction of Dsg3-specific T and B cells upon Dsg3-immunization in the mouse model.
Showed that peptide-bound nanoparticles can efficiently promote Dsg3-specific T cell tolerance, reducing anti-Dsg3 antibody formation.
methods: Used HLA-DRB1*04:02-DQ8 transgenic mice with human CD4 coreceptor and lacking mouse MHC class II (I-Aß-/-).
Immunized mice with recombinant human Dsg3 protein to induce Dsg3-specific CD4+ T and Dsg3-reactive IgG producing B cells.
Characterized low-frequent Dsg3-specific CD4+ T cells using DRB1*04:02 HLA class II Dextramers loaded with immunodominant Dsg3-T cell epitopes.
Analyzed Dsg3-specific B-cells using fluorochrome-labelled Dsg3 and Dsg3-specific B-cell ELISPOT analysis.
Induced Dsg3-specific T cell tolerance by applying a set of immunodominant Dsg3 CD4+ T cell epitopes linked to nanoparticles before Dsg3 immunization.
experiments_or_evidence: Immunization with recombinant human Dsg3 protein led to the induction of Dsg3-specific CD4+ T and Dsg3-reactive IgG producing B cells.
CD4+ T cells were detected after initial immunization and their number decreased over time.
Dsg3-specific B-cells were transiently present in the spleen shortly after Dsg3-immunization but could not be detected at day 21.
At day 21, Dsg3-specific plasma cells were identified in bone marrow using Dsg3-specific B-cell ELISPOT analysis.
Application of peptide-bound nanoparticles reduced anti-Dsg3 antibody formation by 74% and 80% compared to control animals.
assumptions: The mouse model accurately represents the human immune response in PV.
The use of HLA-DRB1*04:02-DQ8 transgenic mice and human Dsg3 protein is a valid approach to study Dsg3-specific immunity.
limitations: The study is preclinical and conducted in a mouse model, which may not fully translate to human conditions.
The long-term efficacy and safety of the nanoparticle-based therapy in humans remain to be determined.

gap_signals.text: The long-term efficacy and safety of the nanoparticle-based therapy in humans remain to be determined.
The study is preclinical and conducted in a mouse model, which may not fully translate to human conditions.
These preclinical proof of concept results hold great promise to translate the concept of Dsg3-specific T cell tolerance induction into the clinic.

Recombinant human Dsg3 protein
DRB1*04:02 HLA class II Dextramers
Fluorochrome-labelled Dsg3
Nanoparticles with immunodominant Dsg3 CD4+ T cell epitopes

keynotes.title: Effect of hypoxic status on survival and therapeutic responses in gastric cancer

keynote.title: Effect of hypoxic status on survival and therapeutic responses in gastric cancer
keynote.core_problem: The influence of hypoxia on the prognosis, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy in gastric cancer (GC) has not been comprehensively studied.
contributions: Development of a prognostic risk assessment model (hypoxia_pred) based on hypoxia-related genes that predicts survival and therapeutic responses in GC.
methods: Clinical information and gene expression profiles were obtained from GEO and TCGA databases.
Unsupervised clustering analysis was performed on a 21-hypoxic genes expression dataset from TCGA-STAD to identify different molecular subtypes of GC.
Prognosis-associated genes were screened using univariate Cox analysis with a p-value cutoff of <0.05.
A prognostic risk model based on 9-genes transcriptome data of both hypoxia and prognosis-related genes was constructed using LASSO regression analysis.
Two additional gene expression datasets from GEO (GSE62254 and GSE26901) were used for model validation.
ESTATE and ssGSEA were used to explore tumor immune infiltration and potential molecular mechanisms of hypoxia in GC.

Survival analysis, targeted drug sensitivity analysis, and assessments of the Cancer Immunome Atlas database were conducted to evaluate the influence of hypoxia on the prognosis, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy of GC patients.
experiments_or_evidence: The AUC for 1- and 3-year overall survival of GC patients in the TCGA cohort were 0.71 and 0.73, respectively, indicating the predictive power of the hypoxia_pred model.
Patients predicted as high-risk by the model had worse immunotherapeutic response and prognosis, suggesting a correlation between hypoxic status and treatment outcomes.

Significant differences in targeted drug sensitivity, immune microenvironment, biological function, immunotherapeutic response, and immune cell infiltration were observed across molecular subtypes and risk groups.
assumptions: Hypoxia is a significant factor in the tumor microenvironment (TME) and is related to cancer metastasis, immune escape, and drug resistance.
limitations: The study relies on retrospective data, which may introduce biases and limit the generalizability of the findings.
The model's performance and its clinical applicability need further validation in prospective studies and clinical trials.

gap_signals.text: The model's performance and its clinical applicability need further validation in prospective studies and clinical trials.
The study relies on retrospective data, which may introduce biases and limit the generalizability of the findings.
The potential prognosis of hypoxia, as well as its influence on the TME, and therapeutic response have not yet been comprehensively studied in gastric cancer (GC).

datasets_or_materials: Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) and The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) datasets, including 375 patients from TCGA-STAD and 409 patients from GEO (GSE62254 and GSE26901)

important_claims.claim: The hypoxia_pred model is a valuable predictor for the survival of GC in the validation cohort (AUC > 0.7).
important_claims.evidence: The AUC for 1- and 3-year overall survival of GC patients in the TCGA cohort were 0.71 and 0.73, respectively.

important_claims.claim: In GC, the aggravation of hypoxia is accompanied by an increase in immune rejection phenotype and the infiltration of immunosuppressive cells.
important_claims.evidence: Significant differences in immune microenvironment and immune cell infiltration were observed in the validation set.

important_claims.claim: Patients predicted as high-risk by the model have worse immunotherapeutic response and prognosis, and may benefit from drugs that inhibit cell cycle signaling pathways.
important_claims.evidence: Validation set results showed that high-risk patients had worse outcomes and suggested potential benefits from specific therapies.

steps.evidence: Significant differences in immune microenvironment and immune cell infiltration were observed in the validation set.

steps.claim: Increase in the infiltration of immunosuppressive cells

interventions: Drugs that inhibit cell cycle signaling pathways

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The hypoxia_pred model provides a theoretical foundation for predicting tumor prognosis and clinical treatment, potentially improving the prognosis of GC patients through personalized immunotherapy recommendations.

keynote.title: Artificial Intelligence for Surgical Scene Understanding: A Systematic Review and Reporting Quality Meta-Analysis
keynote.core_problem: The clinical implementation of surgical scene understanding (SSU) using AI remains limited, despite numerous publications reporting on the potential of AI in identifying instruments, anatomical structures, and other contextual data in surgical imaging.
contributions: Assessment of the current state and research gaps in computational SSU
Highlighting the need for diverse, multi-institutional datasets, robust validation practices, and clinically driven development
methods: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 188 studies from six literature databases

Inclusion criteria: analysis of intraoperative data from minimally invasive abdominal surgeries in humans, development of computational SSU methods, and reporting of trainable models with formal validation and performance metrics
experiments_or_evidence: Most studies relied on small, single-center datasets, often from laparoscopic cholecystectomies, with limited metadata and topical diversity
Research was largely descriptive, with limited reporting on clinical relevance, limitations, code availability, and model uncertainty

Clinical translation was addressed in only eleven works

Improvements in dataset diversity, validation, and clinical focus will enhance the applicability of SSU in real-world settings
limitations: Limited to studies involving minimally invasive abdominal surgeries
Focus on computational SSU methods, excluding other types of AI applications in surgery

keynotes.title: Developing lisocabtagene maraleucel chimeric antigen receptor T-cell manufacturing for improved process, product quality and consistency across CD19+ hematologic indications.

keynote.title: Developing lisocabtagene maraleucel chimeric antigen receptor T-cell manufacturing for improved process, product quality and consistency across CD19+ hematologic indications
keynote.core_problem: Patient-to-patient variability and heterogeneity of starting cellular material across patient populations and disease indications pose challenges to manufacturing consistency in autologous CAR T-cell therapies.
contributions: Optimization of the liso-cel manufacturing platform for improved product quality and consistency despite heterogeneity in starting material
methods: Leukapheresis starting materials were collected from patients with large B-cell lymphoma, mantle cell lymphoma or chronic lymphocytic leukemia treated with liso-cel in clinical trials (NCT02631044 and NCT03331198).

The liso-cel manufacturing process involves selection of CD8+ and CD4+ T cells from leukapheresis material followed by independent activation, transduction, expansion, formulation, and cryopreservation.
Multivariate design of experimental approaches was utilized to optimize process conditions at both specific unit operations and across the process.
Flow cytometry methods were used to assess cellular composition, memory phenotypes, and cell proliferation.

Antigen-specific functions, including cytokine secretion, cytolytic activity, and proliferation, were assessed using endpoint assays after independent stimulation of CD8+ and CD4+ CAR+ T-cell product components.
experiments_or_evidence: Reductions in process duration time, optimization of drug product container and formulation, and activation signal optimization led to significantly increased CAR+ T-cell product viability.

Early T-cell purification reduced the heterogeneity of patient-derived starting material, leading to median T-cell frequencies >95% in selected materials across disease indications and limited non-T-cell impurities.
Correlative analyses demonstrated strong underlying associations between starting material attributes and final CAR+ T-cell product phenotype.
assumptions: The liso-cel manufacturing platform can be optimized to handle diverse starting materials and produce a consistent drug product.
limitations: The study is based on data from specific clinical trials and may not fully represent all possible patient variations or disease states.

gap_signals.text: The study is based on data from specific clinical trials and may not fully represent all possible patient variations or disease states.

Despite substantial heterogeneity of starting leukapheresis material quality/composition between individual patients and across disease indications/histologies, the liso-cel manufacturing platform is robust and capable of generating a consistent drug product from diverse starting materials with a single manufacturing platform.

datasets_or_materials: Leukapheresis starting materials from patients with large B-cell lymphoma, mantle cell lymphoma, or chronic lymphocytic leukemia.

important_claims.claim: The liso-cel manufacturing platform is robust and capable of generating a consistent drug product from diverse starting materials with a single manufacturing platform.
important_claims.evidence: Despite substantial heterogeneity of starting leukapheresis material quality/composition between individual patients and across disease indications/histologies, the liso-cel manufacturing platform is robust and capable of generating a consistent drug product from diverse starting materials with a single manufacturing platform.

Integrates current Good Manufacturing Practice (cGMP) principles and regulatory oversight through a centralized Control Site model.

A centralized Control Site can maintain POCare Master Files and ensure consistency across multiple decentralized sites.

Challenges of obtaining optimal leukapheresis product for CAR T-cell manufacturing include vascular access for smaller patients, achieving sufficient T-cell yield, eliminating contaminating cell types in the leukapheresis product, determining appropriate washout periods for medication, and managing adverse events at collection.

keynotes.title: Bridging preclinical development and clinical manufacturing: a translational GMP-Platform for lentiviral vector production in academic CAR T-Cell therapy

keynote.title: Bridging preclinical development and clinical manufacturing: a translational GMP-Platform for lentiviral vector production in academic CAR T-Cell therapy
keynote.core_problem: The manufacturing of lentiviral vectors (LV) under Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP) is a critical bottleneck limiting the clinical translation of academic CAR T-cell therapies.
contributions: Establishment and validation of an integrated GMP facility (ViPro-IBiS-UPRC) for aseptic LV production within a public healthcare setting.
Development and validation of GMP-compliant production processes and quality control (QC) frameworks for HEK293T Lenti-X master and working cell banks (MCB/WCB) and for LV manufacturing.
methods: Stepwise optimization strategy including refinement of transfection conditions, vector harvest timing, and scale-up surface transition.
GMP-compliant production processes and quality control (QC) frameworks were developed and validated for HEK293T Lenti-X MCB/WCB and LV manufacturing.
experiments_or_evidence: Cell banks demonstrated high viability (≥94%), robust expansion capacity, confirmed identity by DNA fingerprinting, and absence of microbial and viral contaminants, including adventitious and endogenous retroviruses.

GMP-produced LV batches achieved functional titers ranging from 9.95 × 107 to 3.07 × 108 TU/mL and fulfilled all release criteria, including sterility, absence of mycoplasma, endotoxins, and replication-competent lentivirus.
Residual host cell DNA and protein levels remained within international regulatory predefined acceptance thresholds.
Stability studies demonstrated preservation of vector functionality up to 12 months under cryopreservation conditions.

ViPro-IBiS-UPRC obtained GMP certification from the Spanish Agency of Medicines and Medical Devices (AEMPS) for LV production, supporting the manufacture of CARTemis-1, an anti-BCMA CAR T-cell product under clinical evaluation in relapsed multiple myeloma patients.

keynotes.title: Cell therapy manufacturing at full clinical scale: enhancing the quality CAR-T cell therapy starting materials through massively parallel automated microfluidic cell sorting

keynote.title: Cell therapy manufacturing at full clinical scale: enhancing the quality CAR-T cell therapy starting materials through massively parallel automated microfluidic cell sorting
keynote.core_problem: The broader application of autologous CAR-T cell therapy is limited by complex, labor-intensive cell therapy manufacturing and inconsistent product quality.
contributions: Development of a novel microfluidic cell separation platform based on deterministic lateral displacement (DLD) integrated into a fully automated, closed-system instrument (Curate system).
Demonstration of higher leukocyte recovery, better cell subset preservation, and improved T cell expansion compared to conventional methods.
methods: Processing N = 150 leukopacks using the Curate system with a flow rate of 400 mL/h, processed volumes up to 250 mL, and total white blood cell counts up to 24 billion white blood cells.
Comparing the results with Ficoll®-based density gradient centrifugation.
experiments_or_evidence: Leukocyte recovery was significantly higher (88% vs. 58%) with DLD processing compared to Ficoll®-based density gradient centrifugation.
Flow cytometric analysis showed improved phenotypic preservation across key T cell subsets, including naïve and central memory populations.
Cytokine profiling demonstrated enhanced washing efficiency, with markedly lower levels of biologic response modifiers.
DLD-purified T cells exhibited enhanced expansion kinetics and greater yield.
assumptions: The quality and consistency of CAR-T cell therapy can be improved by enhancing the purity and viability of the starting materials.
limitations: The study focuses on the initial stages of CAR-T cell therapy manufacturing, and the long-term clinical outcomes of DLD-processed cells are not evaluated.

gap_signals.text: The study focuses on the initial stages of CAR-T cell therapy manufacturing, and the long-term clinical outcomes of DLD-processed cells are not evaluated.

These findings position microfluidic DLD-based processing as a clinically relevant, scalable alternative to conventional methods, with potential to improve consistency, potency, and accessibility of CAR-T therapies.

Non-parametric algorithm to score Treg manufacturing products for their cell identity and expansion fingerprints.
experiments_or_evidence: The identity fingerprint distinguished Treg from Teff cells with 100% sensitivity and specificity.

keynotes.title: Cytisine for smoking cessation: A systematic review and meta-analysis suggesting potential benefits of extended treatment protocols

keynote.title: Cytisine for smoking cessation: A systematic review and meta-analysis suggesting potential benefits of extended treatment protocols
keynote.core_problem: Determining the optimal treatment duration and dosing pattern for cytisine in smoking cessation, as traditional 25-day tapering regimens may be suboptimal.
contributions: Systematic review and meta-analysis of placebo-controlled trials to evaluate different cytisine treatment durations and dosing patterns.
methods: Systematic review and meta-analysis following PRISMA guidelines (PROSPERO:CRD420251055773).
Search conducted in PubMed, CENTRAL, and Embase from inception to May 14, 2025.
Randomized controlled trials with 3,847 adult smokers aged ≥15 years, comparing cytisine monotherapy with placebo.
Treatment durations included 25-day, 6-week, and 12-week regimens, with dosing patterns categorized as declining-dose or fixed-dose.
experiments_or_evidence: Primary outcome: biochemically-verified continuous abstinence at ≥24 weeks.
Secondary outcomes: severe adverse events.
Risk ratios calculated using random-effects models, with heterogeneity explored through pre-specified subgroup analyses.
assumptions: Cytisine is an effective and affordable smoking cessation aid.
limitations: Substantial heterogeneity in the overall analysis (I2=88.2%).
Meta-regression showed a positive trend in the duration-response relationship, but it was not statistically significant (β=0.069, p=0.217).

gap_signals.text: Meta-regression showed a positive trend in the duration-response relationship, but it was not statistically significant (β=0.069, p=0.217).
Substantial heterogeneity in the overall analysis (I2=88.2%).
Traditional 25-day tapering regimens may be suboptimal, and there is a need to determine the optimal treatment duration and dosing pattern for cytisine.

datasets_or_materials: Data from seven randomized controlled trials conducted in Pakistan, Thailand, Kyrgyzstan, United Kingdom, Italy, and United States.

important_claims.claim: Overall, cytisine significantly increased quit rates (RR=2.99, 95% CI: 1.78-5.00, p<0.001).
important_claims.evidence: Findings from the meta-analysis of seven randomized controlled trials.

important_claims.claim: Twelve-week fixed-dose cytisine regimens nearly double the effectiveness of traditional 25-day protocols while maintaining favorable safety.
important_claims.evidence: Subgroup analysis by duration and dosing pattern, showing RR=3.77 (95% CI: 2.92-4.88) for 12-week regimens and RR=3.71 (95% CI: 2.73-5.05) for fixed-dose regimens.

steps.claim: Increased quit rates compared to traditional 25-day protocols.
steps.evidence: RR=3.77 (95% CI: 2.92-4.88) for 12-week regimens vs. RR=2.00 (95% CI: 0.96-4.17) for 25-day regimens.

steps.claim: Better tolerability in extended regimens.
steps.evidence: Small increase in severe adverse events (RR=1.12, 95% CI: 1.01-1.24), but still within a favorable safety profile.

causal_chains.outcome: More effective and safer smoking cessation treatment.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The findings support updating clinical guidelines to recommend extended fixed-dose cytisine as standard care, offering an affordable alternative to varenicline with comparable efficacy. This can guide future research and clinical practices in smoking cessation.

keynotes.title: Gut microbiota diversity is prognostic and associated with benefit from chemo‐immunotherapy in metastatic triple‐negative breast cancer

keynote.title: Gut microbiota diversity is prognostic and associated with benefit from chemo‐immunotherapy in metastatic triple‐negative breast cancer
keynote.core_problem: Understanding the role of gut microbiota diversity, specifically Faith's phylogenetic diversity (PD), in predicting and improving outcomes for patients with metastatic triple-negative breast cancer (mTNBC) receiving chemo-immunotherapy.
contributions: Demonstrated that high alpha diversity by Faith's PD at baseline is associated with prolonged progression-free survival (PFS) in mTNBC patients.
Identified that high Faith's PD is predictive of benefit from atezolizumab (atezo) when added to chemotherapy.
Found that Bifidobacterium overrepresentation at baseline is associated with lack of clinical benefit in the atezo-chemo arm.
methods: 16S (V3-V4) rRNA sequencing to characterize the diversity and taxonomic composition of the gut microbiota.
Kaplan–Meier and Cox proportional hazard models for time-to-event analyses.
experiments_or_evidence: Fecal samples were collected at baseline and after 8 weeks from patients in the ALICE trial.
High alpha diversity by Faith's PD at baseline was associated with prolonged PFS in the total study population and in the atezo-chemo arm, but not in the placebo-chemo arm.
Patients with high Faith's PD had a PFS hazard ratio of 0.34 (P = 0.018) in favor of the atezo-chemo arm, compared to 0.83 (P = 0.62) in the low Faith's PD group.
assumptions: The gut microbiota influences multiple aspects of human health and disease, including response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in various cancers.
limitations: The study focuses on a specific type of cancer (mTNBC) and may not be generalizable to other types of cancer or patient populations.
The sample size and the specific characteristics of the ALICE trial participants may limit the broader applicability of the findings.

gap_signals.text: The sample size and the specific characteristics of the ALICE trial participants may limit the broader applicability of the findings.
The study focuses on a specific type of cancer (mTNBC) and may not be generalizable to other types of cancer or patient populations.

important_claims.evidence: Kaplan–Meier and Cox proportional hazard models showed a significant association between high Faith's PD and prolonged PFS in the atezo-chemo arm (P = 0.018).

Understanding these principles and challenges is crucial for optimizing antimicrobial therapy and combating AMR through improved dosing strategies.

important_claims.evidence: First, we discuss the importance of drug concentration management for enhancing efficacy while preventing toxicity, considering various patient populations, including pediatric and elderly patients with their unique physiological characteristics.

keynotes.title: Population-Based External Validation of the EASIX Scores to Predict CAR T-Cell-Related Toxicities

keynote.title: Population-Based External Validation of the EASIX Scores to Predict CAR T-Cell-Related Toxicities
keynote.core_problem: The need for reliable and easy-to-use risk scores, such as EASIX and its derivatives, to predict toxicities (CRS and ICANS) associated with CAR T-cell therapy in patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma (r/r LBCL).
contributions: Validation of EASIX, m-EASIX, s-EASIX, and EASIX-F(C) scores in a population-based cohort.
Assessment of the predictive performance of these scores for CRS and ICANS.
methods: Calculation of EASIX, m-EASIX, s-EASIX, and EASIX-F(C) scores at baseline, before lymphodepletion (pre-LD), and at CAR T-cell infusion.
Inclusion of 154 patients with r/r LBCL treated with axicabtagene ciloleucel.
experiments_or_evidence: EASIX and s-EASIX at pre-LD were significantly associated with ICANS grade ≥ 2 (p = 0.04).
EASIX approached statistical significance at infusion (p = 0.05).
Area under the curves (AUC) for EASIX and s-EASIX were 0.61–0.62, indicating moderate performance.
assumptions: The EASIX and its derivatives are potentially useful for predicting toxicities in CAR T-cell therapy.
limitations: Moderate predictive performance of the EASIX scores, requiring further optimization before broad clinical use.
No significant associations between EASIX scores and CRS/ICANS grade ≥ 3.

Moderate predictive performance of the EASIX scores, requiring further optimization before broad clinical use.
Disparities existed among the used endpoints and cutoff values, hampering the utility of these tools in practice.

keynotes.title: Echinocandins Pharmacokinetics: A Comprehensive Review of Micafungin, Caspofungin, Anidulafungin, and Rezafungin Population Pharmacokinetic Models and Dose Optimization in Special Populations

keynote.title: Echinocandins Pharmacokinetics: A Comprehensive Review of Micafungin, Caspofungin, Anidulafungin, and Rezafungin Population Pharmacokinetic Models and Dose Optimization in Special Populations
keynote.core_problem: Understanding and optimizing the pharmacokinetics (PK) of echinocandins (micafungin, caspofungin, anidulafungin, and rezafungin) in special populations to ensure that the PK/PD targets are met, especially in patients who may not achieve these targets with standard dosing.
contributions: Summarized popPK models for micafungin, caspofungin, anidulafungin, and rezafungin.
Focused on dosage optimization to maximize the probability of attaining the PK/PD target in special populations.
methods: Conducted a literature search in PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, and Scopus from inception to March 2024.
Included both observational and prospective trials.
Reviewed 47 studies (22 for micafungin, 13 for caspofungin, 9 for anidulafungin, and 3 for rezafungin).
experiments_or_evidence: A two-compartment model was used in 78.7% of the developed models, though more complex models with three and four compartments were also used.
Weight total (WT) was the most frequently reported significant predictor for both clearance (CL) and volume of distribution (Vd), especially for estimating CL in pediatrics.
assumptions: The PK parameters (CL and Vd) can be estimated using various covariates, with WT being the most significant.
limitations: Lack of external validation of most models.
Significant heterogeneity of the popPK models and study populations.
Limited number of prospective studies.

gap_signals.text: Lack of external validation of most models.
Limited number of prospective studies.
Significant heterogeneity of the popPK models and study populations.
Significant number of studies featured retrospective data, highlighting inherent limitations of this study design.
Due to the lack of external validation, it is advised to carefully select a popPK model that reflects the PK variability of the target population and conduct external validation.

datasets_or_materials: PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, and Scopus databases.

important_claims.claim: Fixed dosing strategies recommended in drug prescribing information may not reach the PK/PD target in certain populations.
important_claims.evidence: In certain populations such as patients that are critically ill, obese, receiving ECMO and/or CRRT, or pediatric patients and/or patients with cancer or that are immunocompromised, the fixed dosing strategies recommended in the drug prescribing information may not reach the PK/PD target.

steps.evidence: In certain populations such as patients that are critically ill, obese, receiving ECMO and/or CRRT, or pediatric patients and/or patients with cancer or that are immunocompromised, the fixed dosing strategies recommended in the drug prescribing information may not reach the PK/PD target.

steps.evidence: For these populations, different strategies have been proposed, such as a dosing regimen based on body weight or increasing the loading and/or maintenance dose.

keynotes.title: Survival Benefit of Temozolomide Plus Irinotecan as Second-Line Therapy in Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Retrospective Single-Center Study

keynote.title: Survival Benefit of Temozolomide Plus Irinotecan as Second-Line Therapy in Small Cell Lung Cancer: A Retrospective Single-Center Study
keynote.core_problem: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) is an aggressive type of cancer with poor prognosis, and current second-line treatments have modest benefits and high toxicity. There is a need for more effective treatment strategies, especially considering the high rate of brain metastases in SCLC patients.
contributions: Evaluation of the clinical outcomes of the combination of temozolomide (TMZ) and irinotecan (IRI) compared to other second-line treatment regimens in relapsed SCLC.
methods: Retrospective review of medical records of 37 patients with relapsed SCLC treated at a tertiary oncology center from January 2018 to December 2023.
Patients were divided into two groups: 24 treated with TMZ+IRI and 13 treated with alternative regimens (topotecan, irinotecan, paclitaxel, docetaxel, vinorelbine, or gemcitabine).
Baseline demographic and clinical data were collected, and survival outcomes (OS and PFS) were estimated using the Kaplan–Meier method and analyzed using Cox regression models.
experiments_or_evidence: Median OS was significantly longer in patients treated with TMZ+IRI compared to controls (25 vs. 8 months, p = 0.002).
One-year OS rates were 58.2% and 25.4%, respectively.

Multivariate analysis identified brain metastases (HR 0.37, 95% CI 0.14–0.95, p = 0.039) and receipt of non-TMZ+IRI regimens (HR 2.82, 95% CI 1.03–7.72, p = 0.044) as independent predictors of poor OS.
Median PFS did not differ significantly between groups (8 vs. 7 months, p = 0.733), and no independent predictors of PFS were identified.
assumptions: The study assumes that the combination of TMZ and IRI, given their pharmacological properties, could be more effective than other second-line treatments, particularly in patients with brain metastases.
limitations: Single-center, retrospective design, which may introduce selection bias and limit generalizability.
Small sample size, which may affect the statistical power and robustness of the findings.

gap_signals.text: Small sample size, which may affect the statistical power and robustness of the findings.
Single-center, retrospective design, which may introduce selection bias and limit generalizability.
Prospective randomized studies are warranted to confirm these results and better define the role of this regimen in treatment sequencing.

datasets_or_materials: Medical records of 37 patients with relapsed SCLC treated at a tertiary oncology center from January 2018 to December 2023.

keynotes.title: Assessing the predictive performance of population pharmacokinetic models for intravenous polymyxin B in critically ill patients

keynote.title: Assessing the predictive performance of population pharmacokinetic models for intravenous polymyxin B in critically ill patients
keynote.core_problem: Dosing of Polymyxin B (PMB) is challenging due to its narrow therapeutic window and pharmacokinetic variability, necessitating accurate population pharmacokinetic (POPPK) models.
contributions: Reviewed published PMB POPPK models and evaluated them using an external validation data set (EVD).
Identified that current POPPK models inadequately predict EVD plasma concentrations.
Suggested the need for a new POPPK model based on an appropriately powered clinical study with an informed PK sampling strategy.
methods: Used seven published POPPK models with reported model equations, parameter values, covariate relationships, interpatient variability, parameter covariance, and unexplained residual variability in NONMEM (Version 7.4.3).
Assessed the predictive ability of the models using prediction-based and simulation-based diagnostics.
experiments_or_evidence: Evaluated the models using an external validation data set (EVD) of 40 critically ill patients from an ongoing clinical study.
Compared patient characteristics and treatment information across studies and with the EVD.
assumptions: Patient characteristics and treatment information are comparable across studies and with the EVD.
Sampling strategy is a main source of PK variability across studies.
limitations: Current POPPK models underpredicted EVD plasma concentrations.
Two-compartment models more accurately described the external data set but still showed limitations.

gap_signals.text: Two-compartment models more accurately described the external data set but still showed limitations.
Current POPPK models underpredicted EVD plasma concentrations.

Creation of a new POPPK model based on an appropriately powered clinical study with an informed PK sampling strategy would be expected to improve characterization of PMB PK and identify covariates to explain interpatient variability.

keynotes.title: Machine learning in paediatric haematological malignancies: a systematic review of prognosis, toxicity and treatment response models

keynote.title: Machine learning in paediatric haematological malignancies: a systematic review of prognosis, toxicity and treatment response models
keynote.core_problem: The application of Machine Learning (ML) in paediatric haematological malignancies is still emerging, necessitating a comprehensive review of its capabilities and limitations.
contributions: Provides a comprehensive overview of current ML applications in paediatric haematological malignancies.
Identifies limitations such as lack of external validation and methodological heterogeneity.
Encourages standardisation and external validation to improve the clinical applicability of ML models.
methods: A literature search was conducted through Ovid.
Studies included focused on ML models in paediatric patients with haematological malignancies.
Studies were categorised into thematic groups for analysis.
experiments_or_evidence: Twenty studies, primarily on leukaemia, were included in this review.
Prognostic studies showed AUC scores between 0.685 and 0.929, indicating moderate-high predictive accuracy.
Treatment response studies demonstrated AUC scores between 0.840 and 0.875, reflecting moderate accuracy.
Toxicity prediction studies reported high accuracy with AUC scores from 0.870 to 0.927.
Only five studies (25%) performed external validation.
limitations: Significant heterogeneity in ML tasks, reporting formats, and effect measures across studies.
Lack of external validation and methodological heterogeneity in many studies.
Limited diversity in datasets, which may affect the generalisability of ML models across different populations.

gap_signals.text: Lack of external validation and methodological heterogeneity in many studies.
Significant heterogeneity in ML tasks, reporting formats, and effect measures across studies.
Limited diversity in datasets, which may affect the generalisability of ML models across different populations.
The clinical applicability of these ML models remains limited by the lack of external validation and methodological heterogeneity.
Addressing these challenges through standardised reporting and rigorous external validation is needed to translate ML from a promising research tool into a reliable clinical practice component.
